observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to validate their records, reconcile any identified reconciling items and effect the necessary adjustments in order to present a reliable balance of the CIB-LCCA account in the financial statements.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated that the Local Chief Executive direct the Inventory Committee, Municipal Accountant, and MGSO to reconcile their respective records to provide accurate information of the PPE accounts.
observations.reason:
Ongoing counting reconciliation
The finding was first raised in CY2022, 2021 & 2019 AAR.

observations.recommendation: a. The Local Chief Executive require the concerned officers and employees who are currently employed with the LGU to liquidate their outstanding cash advances immediately thru submission of liquidation report with supporting documents or pay the amount of the unliquidated cash advance. Likewise, exert extra effort to locate and resend demand letters to those who are no longer connected with the LGU; and b. The Municipal Accountant withhold the salaries of concerned personnel who are currently employed with the LGU who failed to comply with the instructions on liquidation of their long outstanding cash advances as provided under COA Circular No. 97-002.
observations.reason: Immediate liquidation of concerned personnel was not enforced.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the management exert effort to determine whether these unused funds need to be returned to Source Agencies. If found that the funds need to be liquidated, prepare a report to liquidate the unused funds to Source Agencies in compliance with COA Circular No. 94-013 dated December 13, 1994.
observations.reason:
Ongoing analysis of the accounts.
Partial refund made.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Local Chief Executive coordinate with the Source Agency for possible modification of the MOA in order to pursue with the implementation of the projects. Otherwise, revert the fund to the Source Agencies in compliance with the approved Memorandum of Agreement.
observations.reason: DOTR project partial payment made.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the LCE to direct the Inventory Committee LGU to conduct annual physical count of inventories at the end of the year, and prepare the Report of Physical Count of Inventories which shall be submitted to the Auditor not later than January 31 of each year in compliance with Government Accounting Manual for LGUs.
observations.reason:
Agency started only in the MGSO
The finding was first raised in CY 2012, 2010 and 2008 AAR.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor enforce the COA Order of Execution and instruct the Municipal Treasurer to withhold the salaries or other compensation due the persons liable in satisfaction of the disallowance in compliance with COA Circular No. 2009-006 dated September 15, 2009. Also, employees who are retiring or transferring to other agencies shall first settle their disallowances and charges for which they are liable.
observations.reason:
Not all persons liable has settled their disallowances
The finding was first raised in CY 2019 AAR.

observations.recommendation: We recommended the following: a) The Accounting Office and MGSO shall perform reconciliation between their records of Road Networks. Any discrepancies found shall be verified, investigated and adjusted in the books of accounts, if necessary. Henceforth, perform periodic/regular reconciliation of property cards to ensure that all discrepancies are immediately investigated, cleared and reconciled with the books of accounts. The LRN Ledger Card and Property Card shall always be reconciled with each other in compliance COA Circular 2015-008 dated November 23, 2015; b) Municipal General Services Office shall prepare and maintain a Local Road Network Property Card for all roads and its components;
observations.reason:
Report not reconciled.
The finding was first raised in CY 2019 AAR
No Property Card being maintained by the agency.

observations.recommendation: a. The Municipal Accountant shall conduct verification, analysis and validation of the existence of the long unliquidated cash advances, and prepare the necessary adjusting entries for the recognition of the impairment in accordance with the Philippine Public Sector Accounting Standards (PPSAS). b. The Municipal Mayor shall file the request for authority to write-off unliquidated cash advances to the COA Audit Team Leader (ATL) and/or Supervising Auditor (SA) on the following accountable officers/employees in compliance with COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016 with the following requirements: I. For the deceased accountable officers/employees: i. Schedule of dormant accounts by accountable officer/debtor/government entity and by account, certified by the accountant and approved by the Head of the government entity; ii. Death Certificate issued by Philippine Statistics Authority (formerly National Statistics Office) II. For accountable officers/employees that are no longer connected with the LGU with unknown whereabouts: i. Schedule of dormant accounts by accountable officer/debtor/government entity and by account, certified by the accountant and approved by the Head of the government entity; ii. Certificate of no residence in the barangay of the municipality/city of last known address; iii. Certification by Legal Officer of the entity of no pending case relative to the account; and iv. Certification by the responsible officials of the entity to the effect that there are no records/documents available to validate claim.
observations.reason:
Adjusting Entries were not prepared and submitted.
The finding was first raised in CY 2018 AAR.
The auditor has not received any request from the Municipality.

observations.recommendation: We recommended the Municipal Mayor assign personnel temporarily in the accounting office or other offices concerned during preparation and filing of reports for submission to the Office of the Auditor within the prescribed period.

observations.recommendation: We recommended to submit liquidation or fund utilization reports with complete supporting documents to the Office of the Auditor.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Local Chief Executive always ensure that only eligible NGOs/POs will be granted cash advances thru strict compliance to documentary requirements enumerated under COA Circular No. 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012. Also, always ensure that only projects/activities within the capability of the NGO/PO and in line with the SEF priority expenses as provided under DECS-DBM-DILG Joint Circular No. 01, 01-A, and 01-B are granted to NGO/PO. Further, cause the liquidation of the above subject cash advances and stop releasing funds to NGOs/POs with unliquidated cash advances.

Only proposal and Memorandum of Agreement were attached to the voucher.
No documents were submitted to the Office of the Auditor.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Accountant support the Land account with subsidiary ledgers and documents such as certificates of title establishing legal ownership thereto. Management should exercise due diligence in obtaining legal documents and process titling of unsupported real properties owned by the Municipality. The Municipal Accountant should also disclose in the Notes to Financial Statements the composition and valuation of the Land account.
observations.reason:
Preparation of necessary legal documents establishing legal title is still on-going.
The finding was first raised in CY 2014 AAR.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that management require the concerned barangays and the Liga to submit fund utilization reports in accordance with the provisions of the MOA. We recommend that the Municipal Treasurer draw checks in the name of the agency or its head or treasurer. Management should likewise conduct proper planning, budgeting and coordination to avoid delays and other problems that may arise in the execution of its programs, projects and activities.

Liquidation report of Coast Haven Inc. was not submitted
Reiterate to concerned offices to submit immediately the FUR

observations.recommendation: We recommend that the Municipal Accountant, and other concerned department heads and LGU officials in charge of the disbursement and implementation of the funds require the recipient NGO/PO – SAME to comply with the requirements of COA Circular No. 96-003 as amended by COA Circular No. 2007-001 and to ascertain the propriety of expenditures as specified in the Memorandum of Agreement.
observations.reason: SAME has not submitted the required documents.

observations.recommendation:
We recommend that and the Management agreed that:

a.	The Inventory Committee prepare and submit the CY 2023 RPCPPE immediately, and henceforth ensure the timely preparation and submission of the annual RPCPPE;

b.	The Accounting Office and the Inventory Committee reconcile the PPE balances between the RPCPPE and the FS, and the Municipal Accountant present the correct balances in the FS; and

c.	The Accounting Office immediately provide depreciation expenses for the aforementioned undepreciated PPEs using the estimated useful lives and straight-line method of depreciation, in accordance with COA Circular No. 2003-007.

We recommended and the Management agreed to observe the provisions embodied in the Manual on Audit for Fuel Consumption of Government Vehicles.



We also recommended and the Management agreed to prepare and properly fill up the forms and reports prescribed in the manual.

Management prioritizes and ensures the strict implementation of the “One-time cleansing of PPE accounts” in accordance with COA Circular No. 2020-006 by establishing an Inventory Committee comprising an adequate number of committed personnel tasked with spearheading its implementation, with the coordination of the Property Unit/Custodian and Municipal Accountant who shall provide with updated and reconciled balances of the PPE in their respective records.
observations.reason: Salient features of the circular were not yet applied by the management.

observations.recommendation:
Management directs the accountable officers to follow the steps on the relief of accountability from properties pursuant to Sections 151 and 152 of COA Circular 92-386 and submit the request for relief of accountability to COA. 

Shall the request be approved, drop the destroyed assets from the books of accounts by drawing the following journal entry:
observations.reason: On-going gathering of data from the Office of General Services Office (GSO).

observations.recommendation: The Municipal Accountant sends demand letters to the concerned barangays, requesting the immediate submission of the liquidation reports for the funds transferred to them and make the necessary journal entries to take up the expenditures.
observations.reason: Only Barangay Lubang amounting to ₱50,000.00 remained unliquidated as of 12/31/24.

observations.recommendation: The Municipal Accountant discloses in the Notes to Financial Statements the details of the balances of Unexpended LDRRMF as shown in Annex D of COA Circular No. 2012-002 for fair presentation and to enhance the understandability of the users of Financial Statements.
observations.reason: Not reflected in the Notes to Financial Statements of CY 2024.

observations.recommendation: Management complies the necessary forms, registry, and reports in accordance with COA Circular No. 2022-004, including the coordination of the Municipal Accountant and General Services Officer (GSO) to ensure that accountabilities over these properties are established.
observations.reason: Necessary forms, registries and reports were not submitted to COA audit team for review.

observations.recommendation: The Municipal Mayor and Municipal Budget Officer adhere with the mandates of RA No. 7160 and discontinue the practice of allowing MOOE items to be included in the continuing appropriations. Remaining balances classified as MOOE in the continuing appropriations as of December 31, 2023, shall be reverted to the unappropriated surplus of the general fund which shall thereafter be available for expenditure through subsequent enactment. Moreover, projects funded out of the 20% development fund only include items that shall partake the nature of investment or capital expenditures in consonance to DILG-DBM JMC No. 2017-1 and 2020-1, dated February 22, 2017 and March 27, 2020, respectively.
observations.reason: Not capital outlay items were still included in the 20% Development Fund budget.

observations.recommendation: The Municipal officials concerned prioritize and expedite the implementation of projects funded out of the 20% Development Fund for CY 2023. They shall also reassess and reconsider the feasibility of budgeted projects that are difficult to implement, allocating funds only to projects that are ready for procurement and implementation in the upcoming years.
observations.reason: The projects/programs were not yet completely implemented.

observations.recommendation: The list of all ongoing government PPAs and those that are to be implemented for the ensuing year and the corresponding monthly/quarterly monitoring reports are submitted within the prescribed period in COA Circular No. 2013-004 dated January 30, 2013, as amended by COA Circular No. 2015-006 dated August 20, 2015.
observations.reason: Management is still improving the filing system in order to have an up-to-date submission of reports.

observations.recommendation: Management requires the Inventory Committee to prioritize and complete the physical inventory within a reasonable period in accordance with COA Circular No. 2020-006. Management is also recommended to ensure the full implementation of “One-time cleansing of PPE accounts” as provided for in the same circular.
observations.reason: Salient features of the circular were still not applied by management.

observations.recommendation: Management directs the Inventory Committee together with the Appraisal/Disposal Committee to process the IIRUP for disposal of unserviceable properties and submit to the COA Auditor pursuant to COA Circular No. 2020-006 and Section 79 of PD No. 1445. Copy of approved IIRUP shall be furnished to the COA. However, the Accounting Office shall not drop the approved IIRUP from the accounting records until such time the applicable disposal procedure is complete.
observations.reason: Non-action from the GSO.

observations.recommendation: The Municipal Engineering Office immediately submits/provides a copy of the issued certificate of completion together with the supporting documents every after-project completion to the Accounting Office. Upon receipt, the latter shall immediately reclassifies/ transfers the same from the Construction in Progress account to the appropriate PPE account and thereafter start the provision of depreciation. During the reclassification, the Accounting Office recognizes Accounts Payable and reverse the same whenever there is already full payment being made.
observations.reason: Corrected Quarterly Report on Government Projects were not yet submitted to the audit team.

observations.recommendation: Management observes and complies the necessary requirements prior to the disposal of its properties as required under COA Circular No. 92-386. Also, submit the necessary documentary requirements as well as justification on the non-compliance on the procedure on disposal of properties.
observations.reason: No documentary requirements were submitted by management to the audit team.

observations.recommendation: Management stops the practice of granting/approving work suspension and contract time extensions for reasons other than what is enumerated in RA 9184. Also require the Municipal Engineer to submit explanation/justification as to why the request for suspension and contract time extension for Suspension Order No.1 and 2 were granted. Otherwise, liquidated damages have to be collected from the contractor.
observations.reason: Management has not yet resubmitted the explanation/justification to the audit team.

observations.recommendation: Management requires the Municipal Budget Officer to submit the SAAOB on the 20th day of the following month to facilitate the verification of transactions and reports of the LGU.
observations.reason: Management only submitted the SAAOB at year-end upon request by the audit team.

observations.recommendation: Management submits a valid justification letter for the procurement of materials in excess of needed quantity which is considered unconscionable expenditure as cited under Annex F of COA Circular No. 2012-003 dated October 29, 2012, otherwise may cause the issuance of suspension or disallowance in audit.
observations.reason: Management has not yet resubmitted the explanation/justification to the audit team.

observations.recommendation: Management follows up the production of the SB Resolution requesting for the write-off of these dormant accounts together with the recommendation of the Local Chief Executive. Likewise, the Municipal Accountant issues certifications that the municipality has no more records or information, hence the chance to collect is no longer attainable.
observations.reason: Accounts were still not written-off as of 12/31/25 due to the non-submission of documents.

observations.recommendation: The Municipal Assessor prioritizes the registration and transfers of title of the land to ensure ownership and to fully protect the interest of the Municipality in compliance with Section 39 of PD No.1445. Also, the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Assessor need to reconcile their records of the land owned by the LGU.
observations.reason: Still on-going processing of documents.

observations.recommendation: Management creates a GAD Focal Point System to ascertain the alignment of GAD agenda and propose activities identified as gender issues. Also, establishes GAD database/sex-disaggregated data to come up with an evidenced-base programs, activities/projects intended for gender causes. Moreover, it maximizes the utilization of GAD funds in order to attain the objective for which the funds were provided.
observations.reason: Still on-going establishment of GAD database by management.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Management require the Municipal
Accountant (MA) and AMT to:
a) exert utmost effort in obtaining the supporting documents
necessary for the reconciling items and draw journal entries to take
up the reconciling items;
b) undertake the reconciliation of the General Ledger and the
Cashbook, on a regular basis, to ensure prompt, accurate and
complete recording of journal entries;
c) prepare and submit the BRS on a regularly and timely manner in
accordance with Section 74 of PD No. 1445 and Section 3 of COA
Circular No. 96-011 dated October 2, 1996; and
d) submit immediately to the Audit Team the DVs and supporting
documents pertaining to the three checks that have already been
debited from the bank but have yet to be recorded in the accounting
books, in order to avoid the issuance of Notice of Suspension.

We recommended that the Management require the:
a) MTO to furnish the MAO, on periodic basis, the necessary
report/document pertinent to collectibles including the penalties
and surcharges from the operation of waterworks system for
recording purposes; and
b) MAO to make the necessary adjustment in the books for the
unrecorded receivables from unpaid water service fees.

We reiterated our recommendations that the Management instruct the
MA to a) conduct regular and periodic verification, analysis and validation of
the existence and valuation of their receivables; b) prepare the necessary
adjusting entries to recognize impairment losses for receivables which has
uncertainty of collectability or the amount which has ceased to be probable as
required under IPSAS 29; and c) request authority to write-off the dormant
accounts receivables which have been outstanding for ten or more years in
accordance with COA Circular No. 2016-005.

We reiterated our recommendations that the Management instruct the
MA to (i) effect the necessary adjustments after reconciliation to reflect the
correct amounts of receivable, payable and income accounts in the books at
year-end; (ii) analyze all the outstanding balances that are dormant, long
outstanding and unsubstantiated for proper disposition and de-recognition, if
warranted; and (iii) communicate with source LGUs and perform
reconciliation to ascertain the nature of the transferred funds, whether the
funds received require liquidation or are to be treated as subsidy to the LGU
otherwise, prepare and submit liquidation reports for the expended portion
and cause the return to the source agencies of all unutilized balances of the
fund transfers.

We reiterated our recommendations to the Management to instruct the
MA to:
a) trace the causes of the noted unreconciled balances between the
Intra-Agency receivables Due from Other Funds and Intra-
Agency Payables Due to Other Funds accounts;
b) take up the necessary adjustments to effect the reconciliation of the
affected accounts; and
c) closely monitor/review the transactions recorded into these
accounts in order to prevent any unreconciled balances in the
future.

We recommended that the Management require the MA to draw
adjusting journal entries to correct the erroneous recording of cash bond paid
by the Municipality pertinent to their application for land conversion use of a
parcel of land situated in Barangay Caigangan, Buenavista.

We recommended the Management to:
a) compel the accountable officers including the MA, AMT and other
concerned employees to immediately liquidate their cash advances
within the prescribed reglementary period otherwise, withhold the
payment of money due to these officers and employees as provided
under Section 5.1.3 of COA Circular No. 97-002 dated February 10,
1997 or demand full payment of the amount advanced to them
specially those who had already separated from service;
b) instruct the MA to stop granting of additional cash advances to
concerned officials and employees unless the previous cash
advances granted to them had already been liquidated;
c) remind the AMT against drawing of cash advances in excess of his
allowable maximum cash accountability; and
d) instruct the MA to i) conduct regular and periodic verification,
analysis and validation of the existence and valuation of their cash
advances; ii) prepare the necessary adjusting entries to recognize
impairment losses for cash advances which has uncertainty of
collectability or the amount which has ceased to be probable as
required under IPSAS 29; and iii) request authority to write-off the
dormant cash advances which have been outstanding for ten or
more years in accordance with COA Circular No. 2023-008.

We recommended that the Management require the:
a) IC to conduct physical count of inventories and to prepare and
submit with complete details the updated Report on Physical Count
of Inventories (RPCI) in accordance with Section 114, Volume I,
and Section 56, Volume II, of the MNGAS; and
b) MA to i) recognize as expense when the goods are issued/distributed
or related service is rendered in the period to which they relate in
adherence with paragraph 37 of IPSAS 12; ii) prepare the
appropriate journal entries to (a) recognize the materials recorded
in the Construction Materials Inventory account for the completed
infrastructure projects to its proper PPE account; (b) derecognize
the distributed/consumed inventories totaling ₱2,131,374.00 and (c)
adjust the misclassified inventories totaling ₱1,302,352.00 to its
proper accounts; and iii) comply strictly with the proper
classification of inventory accounts prescribed under the
RCALGU.

We recommended that the Management:
a) compel the Municipal Inventory Committee to conduct actual physical
inventory count and to fully accomplish the RPCPPE indicating the actual
count and complete details of the report; and
b) instruct the MA to i) exert effort to properly identify the details of
unidentified PPE items totaling ₱30,234,928.58 recorded in the PPE
Lapsing Schedule in order to verify the existence thereof; ii) prepare the
appropriate journal entries and effect the necessary adjustments to the Land
and Other Maintenance and Operating Expenses accounts; iii) draw journal
entries to close the completed infrastructure projects to its corresponding
PPE accounts; and iv) reconcile with the Inventory Committee the balances
of the PPE records as against the RPCPPE.

We recommended the Management to instruct the:
a) MA to prepare the necessary adjusting journal entry to recognize
the unremitted GSIS premium contributions totaling ₱484,464.76;
and
b) AMT to (i) remit in full all GSIS contributions or payments
withheld from accounts of employees on or before the prescribed
due dates in accordance with Sections 14 and 15 of the IRR of RA
No. 8291; and (ii) strictly monitor the remittance of withheld
amounts to prevent accumulation and incurrence of unnecessary
penalties.

We reiterated our recommendations to the Management to:
a) require the MA and the AMT to immediately effect the remittance
of the unexpended balances to the concerned source agency as
required under Sections 4.9 and 6.7 of COA Circular No. 94-013
and Section 122 of GAAM, Volume I; and
b) perform reconciliation with source agencies to ascertain the
nature of the transferred funds, whether the funds received
require liquidation or are to be treated simply as subsidy to the
LGU; if due for liquidation, prepare and submit liquidation
reports for the expended portion and cause the return to the
source agencies of all unutilized balances of the fund transfers.

We recommended that the Management:
a) instruct the MA to use the appropriate accounts to record retention
and prepare adjusting entries to correct the Guaranty/Security
Deposits Payable and Other payables accounts;
b) ensure that future procurements be deducted with at least 1% but
not more than 5% retention money or require a special bank
guarantee of the same amount as required under Section 62.1 of the
Revised IRR of RA No. 9184, otherwise any expenses due to the
defects within the warranty period be charged to concerned
officials; and
c) require the retention of ten percent (10%) for every progress
payment of Infrastructure projects based on the total amount due to
the contractor prior to any deduction or an irrevocable standby
letters of credit from a commercial bank, bank guarantees or surety
bonds callable on demand, of amounts equivalent to the retention
money substituted for and acceptable to the Government, provided
that the project is on schedule and satisfactorily undertaken in
compliance with Section 6 of Annex “E” of the Revised IRR of RA
No. 9184.

We reiterated our recommendations that the Management:
a) Take the necessary actions to expedite the collection of the
unsettled disallowances amounting to ₱8,215,722.78; and
b) Cause all the liable and certifying approving officers, who took part in
the disbursement of disallowed expenditures, to assume
responsibility in the final settlement of the remaining balance of the
disallowance; and
c) Require the Municipal Accountant to (i) record the excess payment of
₱2,048.87 from the cash shortage as the excess payment other income
by debiting Receivables - Disallowances/Charges account and
crediting Miscellaneous Income account; (ii) ensure that before
allowing settlement of disallowances through installment payment the
same was authorized and approved by COA; and (iii) submit to the
Office of the Auditor a schedule containing partial settlement of
disallowances of each liable persons and the corresponding Journal
Entry Voucher for the issuance of notice of settlement; and (iv)
ensure that all receivables- disallowances reported in the books are
valid.

We reiterated and the Management agreed that the Inventory Committee,
Municipal Accountant, and MGSO to reconcile their respective records to
provide accurate information of the PPE accounts.

We recommended and the Management agreed to the following:
a. Make the necessary adjusting entry in the general fund books using the
suggested entry
Debit - Transfer of unspent DRRM Fund ₱5,061,276.72
Credit - Trust Liability – DRRMF ₱5,061,276.72
To record unspent DRRMF
b. Transfer the unspent DRRM Fund to Trust Fund account in compliance
with Section 6.4 of National Disaster Risk Reduction and Management
Council (NDRRMC), DBM and DILG Joint Memorandum Circular No.
2013-1 dated March 25, 2013 and COA Circular No. 2012-02 dated
September 12, 2012

We recommended and the Management agreed to make the following
adjusting entries:
MEEDO Fund:
PY Adjustment 21,000.00
Allowance on Impairment 21,000.00
Semi-expendable (Furniture & Fixture) 42,000.00
Special Education Fund:
PY Adjustment 171,785.00
Semi-expendable (ICT Equipment) 171,785.00

We recommended that the Management agreed to:
a. Conduct physical count of inventory of supplies and materials and submit
the required inventory report to COA in compliance with Section 124 of
the New Government Accounting Manual for LGUs, Volume I; and
b. Accounting Office to comply with the proper recording of supplies and
materials as prescribed under Section 124 of the New Government
Accounting Manual for LGUs, Volume I.

We recommended and the Management agreed to create or assign the RC
and RC Code or maintain a subsidiary ledger for GAD in compliance with
COA Circular 2021-008 dated September 06, 2021.

We recommend that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Human Resource
Management Officer and the Accounting Office to strictly monitor and see to
it that monthly net take home pay of employees shall not be less than the
required minimum monthly net take home pay pursuant to the provisions of
the succeeding General Appropriations Act.

Also, any deficiency in loan repayments and other obligations as a result of the
mandatory minimum requirement may be addressed through direct payment
arrangements to be voluntarily made by the employees to the concerned
institutions.

We recommended to the Municipal Accountant and agreed to:
Submit a reconciliation report of the intra-agency receivables and intra-agency payables variance of ₱2.815 million, otherwise, exhaust efforts to reconcile the discrepancy; and
Eliminate the reciprocal accounts during the preparation of the financial statements recognizing assets and liabilities in accordance with Paragraph 7 of IPSAS 1.

We recommended and the Accounting Office agreed to ensure correct depreciation computation by providing depreciation once the asset is available for use.
We also recommended and the Accounting Office agreed to reclassify PPE amounting to less than ₱50,000.00 as semi-expendable property in accordance with the provisions of COA Circular No. 2022-004.

We recommended and the Management agreed to facilitate the disposal of unserviceable properties following the procedures set forth in COA Circular No. 92-386 to prevent further deterioration and to realize income therefrom if found to be saleable.
We also recommended and the Management agreed to assess the recoverable service amount of unserviceable properties in accordance with the guidelines in IPSAS 17 and 21 and that the Accounting Office record the appropriate derecognition or impairment costs to fairly present the asset’s carrying value in the financial statements.

We recommended and the Management agreed to observe the provisions embodied in the Manual on Audit for Fuel Consumption of Government Vehicles.
We also recommended and the Management agreed to prepare and properly fill up the forms and reports prescribed in the manual.

We recommended and the Human Resource Management Officer or responsible personnel submit within five working days from execution/issuance the JOCs and their supporting documents for review by the Auditor.

We recommended that the GSO-designate conduct the physical count of all the properties recorded in the books and those not yet recorded but actually found in the LGU offices/premises. Likewise, prepare the corresponding inventory reports thereof in accordance with the prescribed format of the NGAS Manual and submit to the Auditor, at least annually, after reconciliation with accounting records as provided by law and regulations.
We recommended that the Municipal Engineer’s Office submit corrected Quarterly Report on Government Projects to the audit team.

We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer provide the Municipal Accountant with an updated and reconciled list of delinquent real property taxes to facilitate a detailed reconciliation of the unreconciled amount of ₱1,142,469.41, identify specific discrepancies, and make the necessary adjustments, including rectifying the excess inclusion of ₱902,917.39 from prior years to prevent duplication by drawing the following adjusting entries:

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant correct the misclassified amounts to the appropriate liability account through the preparation of the following proposed journal entry to ensure a fair presentation of the financial statements:
We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant conduct a thorough review of prior period transactions to identify and correct any historical misclassifications.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant prepare the following adjusting entries:
We recommended that, henceforth, future transfers of the unexpended balance of the LDRRMF be limited to the 30% Quick Response Fund (QRF) and the 70% Maintenance and Other Operating Expenses (MOOE) only.

We recommended the Municipal Accountant to adopt the suggested pro forma journal entry for recording Accounts Payable at year-end to minimize the risk of administrative penalties and ensure the accuracy of the financial statements:

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant and Agricultural Officer prepare and maintain the ledger cards and stock/property cards respectively, for the Breeding Stocks and reconcile their balances regularly. We further recommend that the Municipal Agriculture Officer (MAO) regularly monitor the condition of the livestock/breeding stocks under his supervision and update his records for easy reference. Finally, we recommend that the inventory committee conduct a physical count of the reported Breeding Stocks and submit the Report not later than January 31 of each year.
We recommended that the appropriate account, specifically Advances to Contractors, be used for recording mobilization costs availed by contractors, in full adherence to COA Circular 2015-009.

We recommended that management return all unused balances of fund transfers to the SAs to comply with the provisions stated in Item 6.7 of COA Circular 94-013, dated December 13, 1994 or seek authority from the issuing agency for the utilization of the excess funds.

We recommended that management, in coordination with all Department Heads responsible for supervising and monitoring specific programs, projects, and activities (PPAs), ensure the prompt implementation of any remaining unimplemented PPAs and submit the complete fund utilization reports in compliance with COA Circular No. 94-013.
Furthermore, for PPAs that have already been implemented but remain unliquidated, we recommended that management expedite the submission of liquidation reports to clear the outstanding balances in the liability accounts and ensure the accuracy and reliability of the financial statements.

We recommended that management make the necessary revisions to the GAD Plan and Budget in the succeeding years to ensure that the total cost of related activities is at least 5% of the total budget appropriations and consider allocating a supplemental budget to correct any deficiency in budgeting requirements in compliance with Section 36(a) of Republic Act (RA) 9710, The Magna Carta of Women.

We recommended that Management require the GAD Focal Person to prepare and submit the GAD Accomplishment Report for CY 2024 to the COA Auditor. Further, enforce strict compliance with the provisions of COA Circular No. 2014-001 on the submission of the annual GAD Accomplishment Report.

We recommended and the Management agreed that:
a. The Municipal General Services Office furnish the Municipal
Accountant copy of the duly approved Inventory and Inspection
Report of Unserviceable Properties (IIRUP) and other pertinent
documents on disposed unserviceable properties in CY 2024; and
b. The Municipal Accountant prepare the necessary adjusting journal
entry derecognizing the carrying value of the disposed
unserviceable properties, as follows:
Accumulated Depreciation ₱ 39,912,835.00
Prior Period Adjustment 4,435,315.00
Construction & Heavy Equipment ₱ 34,445,000.00
Motor Vehicles 9,903,150.00
To record the derecognition of the disposed unserviceable properties.

We recommended and the Management agreed that the Municipal
Accountant prepare the necessary adjusting journal entry to correct
the PPE account balance as follows:
Buildings ₱268,950.00
Prior Period Adjustment ₱268,950.00
To correct the erroneous entry for recording the labor cost to
outright expense to the appropriate PPE Account

We recommended and the Management agreed that:
a. Various departments submit the final list of properties under their
custody to the GSO for consolidation and preparation of the
RPCPPE;
b. The Inventory Committee prepare the Physical Inventory Plan in
June 2025 to plan the conduct of physical count of all properties
listed in the RPCPPE and efficiently comply with the one-time
cleansing of PPEs;
c. The GSO and the Municipal Accountant prepare and maintain the
Property Cards and PPE Ledger Cards, respectively, to each class
of property, plant and equipment in calendar year 2025; and
d. The GSO and the Municipal Accountant to conduct the
reconciliation of the RPCPPE and PPE Ledger Cards in calendar
year 2025.

We recommended and the management agreed to designate a Project
Monitoring Team in calendar year 2025 that will closely monitor
project progress and address any issues affecting the timely
implementation of projects funded by National Government Agencies
and provide a detailed implementation schedule to prevent
unnecessary delays. If delays continue, a request should be made
for the return of funds to the source agencies.

We recommended and the management agreed that the Local School
Board:
a. Furnish the Municipal Budget Officer, Municipal Accountant, and
Municipal Treasurer within seven (7) days after the approval of the
SEF Budget and properly document its receipt; and
b. Submit to the DepEd Central Office through the appropriate
channels, the Local Sanggunian of the Municipality, and the
regional offices of DBM and DILG the quarterly and annual reports
on SEF Budget Accountability Form No. 1 on SEF utilization.

We recommended and the management agreed that:
a. The end-user offices together with the General Services Office
designate personnel that will navigate, input data and generate
reports from the General Services Management System; and
b. The Local Chief Executive request the Systems and Plan
Integrator and Development Corporation (SPIDC) to conduct in-
depth training to designated personnel from end-user offices on
how to navigate the various modules installed in the General
Services Management System and to prepare the operational
manual for specific guidance to users.

We recommended and the management agreed that the GAD Focal
Point System Secretariat:
a. Prepare the GAD Agenda with consultation to local functionaries
and stakeholders at the beginning of term of leadership of the Local
Chief Executive.
b. Conduct Gender Analysis/Assessment during the formulation of
PPAs and re-evaluate the target beneficiaries using the sex-
disaggregated data/information to ensure that Gender issues/gaps
were addressed.

We recommended that the LCE secures the authority from the Sangguiang Bayan for the utilization of the lumpsum appropriation for Barangay Development Project and Infrastructure Support Project activities appropriated under the 20% Development Fund. We further recommended that the Management exercise prudence in the preparation of the executive budget and comply with the requirements by the Budget Operations Manual prior to submission to the Sanggunian for authorization through enactment of Appropriation Ordinance.

Evaluate continuing appropriation if projects are still viable to be implemented and recommend result of evaluation to the Municipal Developmental Council for proper discharge accruing to developmental needs of the Municipality; and Declare the remaining amount as savings from completed projects and submit such to the Sangunian for appropriation.
observations.reason: Evaluate continuing appropriation if projects are still viable to be implemented and recommend result of evaluation to the Municipal Developmental Council for proper discharge accruing to developmental needs of the Municipality; and Declare the remaining amount as savings from completed projects and submit such to the Sangunian for appropriation

observations.recommendation: Stop the charging expenses against the LDRRM Fund not allowed in compliance with Section 5 of NDRRMC, DBM and DILG Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-1 dated March 25, 2013 and NDRRMC, DILG, DBM and CSC Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2014-1 dated April 4, 2014. Restore to the LDRRM Fund the amount representing disbursements in CY 2023 that were erroneously charged against the fund which shall be re-appropriated to finance only those programs, projects and activities identified to be funded under the 5% LDRRM Fund for the ensuing year.
We recommended that the Municipal Government adopt a system of payroll payment through the ATMs of banks.

We recommended and the Municipal Treasurer agreed to diligently monitor stale checks and communicate with payees for their timely encashment.

We also recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to make the necessary adjusting entries regarding the outstanding checks.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the concerned municipal officials/employees to immediately submit their liquidation reports and related documents or refund their cash advances.

We also recommend that Accountable Officers ensure that all cash advances are liquidated within the specified period after the purpose of its grant has been served and/or before the end of each year.
We recommended and the Management agreed to stop the practice of charging to the LDRRMF expenses which are not enumerated in the aforesaid circular.

We recommended and the Management agreed to direct the General Services Officer or responsible personnel to submit within five working days from execution/issuance the Purchase Order and all the supporting documents for review of the Auditor.

The Municipal Accountant prepares adjusting journal entries for the aforementioned book reconciling items indicated in the BRS to ensure an accurate balance of the Cash in Bank account. To prevent the occurrence and accumulation of unadjusted book reconciling items in the future, there is timely preparation and submission of monthly bank reconciliation statements.

To enhance transparency and facilitate effective monitoring of the allocation of the LDRRMF, the Municipal Accountant implements sub-codes within the Special Accounts for this will provide a structured system to categorize and track the allocation of funds, promoting clearer financial reporting and facilitating better oversight of LDRRM Fund utilization.

Management refrains from granting additional funds to the barangays with unliquidated fund transfers. The municipal accountant provides the aforementioned certification only to implementing agencies with liquidated and properly accounted funds.

The Accounting and Treasury Office regularly coordinates regarding the identification and management of stale checks. To prevent the occurrence of stale checks in the future, the Municipal Treasurer shall issue written notices to the concerned payees at least one month before the checks become stale. Further, the Municipal Accountant shall make the necessary and timely adjustments in the books of accounts.

Management updates their Monthly Report of Accountability for Accountable Forms (RAAF) and ensure to account all accountable forms with or without money value, including checks for their various bank accounts.
Subsequent cash advances are supported with a Physical and Financial Plan, whose total budget is equal to the amount of cash advance, serving as the basis or disbursements.

We recommended and the OIC-Municipal Accountant agreed to prepare journal entry vouchers to recognize the adjustment of the Construction in Progress account for completed projects to the appropriate PPE accounts. In addition, recognize the unrecorded depreciation to the Prior Period Adjustment account to present fairly the Government Equity account of the Municipality.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated that the Local Chief Executive direct the Inventory Committee, Municipal Accountant, and MGSO to prepare the Report on the Physical Count of PPE (RPCPPE) and reconcile the same and submit to the Auditor concerned not later than January 31 of each year.

We reiterated the following:

a.	The Local Chief Executive require the concerned officers and employees who are currently employed with the LGU to liquidate their outstanding cash advances immediately thru submission of liquidation report with supporting documents or pay the amount of the unliquidated cash advance.  Likewise, exert extra effort to locate and resend demand letters to those who are no longer connected with the LGU; and

b.	The Municipal Accountant withhold the salaries of concerned personnel who are currently employed with the LGU who failed to comply with the instructions on liquidation of their long outstanding cash advances as provided under COA Circular No. 97-002.

We recommended that the management exert effort to determine whether these unused funds need to be returned to Source Agencies. If found that the funds need to be liquidated, prepare a report to liquidate the unused funds to Source Agencies in compliance with COA Circular No. 94-013 dated December 13, 1994.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive coordinate with the Source Agency for possible modification of the MOA in order to pursue with the implementation of the projects. Otherwise, revert the fund to the Source Agencies in compliance with the approved Memorandum of Agreement.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor enforce the COA Order of Execution and instruct the Municipal Treasurer to withhold the salaries or other compensation due the persons liable in satisfaction of the disallowance in compliance with COA Circular No. 2009-006 dated September 15, 2009.  Also, employees who are retiring or transferring to other agencies shall first settle their disallowances and charges for which they are liable.

We recommended that:

a.	The Municipal Accountant shall conduct verification, analysis and validation of the existence of the long unliquidated cash advances, and prepare the necessary adjusting entries for the recognition of the impairment in accordance with the Philippine Public Sector Accounting Standards (PPSAS).

b.	The Municipal Mayor shall file the request for authority to write-off unliquidated cash advances to the COA Audit Team Leader (ATL) and/or Supervising Auditor (SA) on the following accountable officers/employees in compliance with COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016 with the following requirements:

I.	For the deceased accountable officers/employees:

i.	Schedule of dormant accounts by accountable officer/debtor/government entity and by account,  certified by the accountant and approved by the Head of the government entity;
ii.	Death Certificate issued by Philippine Statistics Authority (formerly National Statistics Office)

II.	For accountable officers/employees that are no longer connected with the LGU with unknown whereabouts:

i.	Schedule of dormant accounts  by accountable officer/debtor/government entity and by account,  certified by the accountant and approved by the Head of the government entity;
ii.	Certificate of no residence in the barangay of the  municipality/city of last known address;
iii.	Certification by Legal Officer of  the entity of no pending case relative to the account; and 
iv.	Certification by the responsible officials of the entity to the effect that there are no records/documents available to validate claim.

We recommended the Municipal Mayor assign personnel temporarily in the accounting office or other offices concerned during preparation and filing of reports for submission to the Office of the Auditor within the prescribed period.
We recommended that the Municipal Government adopt a system of payroll payment through the ATMs of banks.
We recommended to submit liquidation or fund utilization reports with complete supporting documents to the Office of the Auditor.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive always ensure that only eligible NGOs/POs will be granted cash advances thru strict compliance to documentary requirements enumerated under COA Circular No. 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012.  Also, always ensure that only projects/activities within the capability of the NGO/PO and in line with the SEF priority expenses as provided under DECS-DBM-DILG Joint Circular No. 01, 01-A, and 01-B are granted to NGO/PO. Further, cause the liquidation of the above subject cash advances and stop releasing funds to NGOs/POs with unliquidated cash advances.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant support the Land account with subsidiary ledgers and documents such as certificates of title establishing legal ownership thereto. Management should exercise due diligence in obtaining legal documents and process titling of unsupported real properties owned by the Municipality. The Municipal Accountant should also disclose in the Notes to Financial Statements the composition and valuation of the Land account.

We recommended that management require the concerned barangays and the Liga to submit fund utilization reports in accordance with the provisions of the MOA. We recommend that the Municipal Treasurer draw checks in the name of the agency or its head or treasurer. Management should likewise conduct proper planning, budgeting and coordination to avoid delays and other problems that may arise in the execution of its programs, projects and activities.
We recommended to require the Binirayan Foundation, Inc. to submit immediately the fund utilization report copy furnished the Office of the Auditor.

We recommend that LGU officials, the Municipal Accountant and other concerned personnel in charge of the disbursement and monitoring of the financial assistance should require the recipient NGO/PO– Association of Tiringbanay Cooperators Inc. and Coast Haven Inc. to comply with the requirements of COA Circular No. 2007-001 to ascertain the attainment of objectives for which the funds were released as well as the propriety of expenditures.

We recommend that the Municipal Accountant, and other concerned department heads and LGU officials in charge of the disbursement and implementation of the funds require the recipient NGO/PO – SAME to comply with the requirements of COA Circular No. 96-003 as amended by COA Circular No. 2007-001 and to ascertain the propriety of expenditures as specified in the Memorandum of Agreement.

We recommended and the Management agreed to direct the General Services Officer or responsible personnel to submit within five working days from execution/issuance the Purchase Order and all the supporting documents for review of the Auditor.

We recommended that management require the Inventory Committee to prioritize and complete the physical inventory within a reasonable period in accordance with COA Circular No. 2020-006. Management is also recommended to ensure the full implementation of “One-time cleansing of PPE accounts” as provided for in the same circular.

We recommend that management direct the Inventory Committee together with the Appraisal/Disposal Committee to process the IIRUP for disposal of unserviceable properties and submit to the COA Auditor pursuant to COA Circular No. 2020-006 and Section 79 of PD No. 1445. Copy of approved IIRUP shall be furnished to the COA. However, the Accounting Office shall not drop the approved IIRUP from the accounting records until such time the applicable disposal procedure is complete.

We recommend that the Municipal Engineering Office immediately submit/provide a copy of the issued certificate of completion together with the supporting documents every after-project completion to the Accounting Office. Upon receipt, the latter shall immediately reclassify/ transfer the same from the Construction in Progress account to the appropriate PPE account and thereafter start the provision of depreciation. During the reclassification, the Accounting Office may recognize Accounts Payable and reverse the same whenever there is already full payment being made.

We recommend that Management observe and comply the necessary requirements prior to the disposal of its properties as required under COA Circular No. 92-386. Also, submit the necessary documentary requirements as well as justification on the non-compliance on the procedure on disposal of properties.

We recommend that management stop the practice of granting/approving work suspension and contract time extensions for reasons other than what is enumerated in RA 9184. Also require the Municipal Engineer to submit explanation/justification as to why the request for suspension and contract time extension for Suspension Order No.1 and 2 were granted. Otherwise, liquidated damages have to be collected from the contractor.
We recommend that management require the Municipal Budget Officer to submit the SAAOB on the 20th day of the following month to facilitate the verification of transactions and reports of the LGU.

We recommend that management submit a valid justification letter for the procurement of materials in excess of needed quantity which is considered unconscionable expenditure as cited under Annex F of COA Circular No. 2012-003 dated October 29, 2012, otherwise may cause the issuance of suspension or disallowance in audit.

We recommend that management should follow up the production of the SB Resolution requesting for the write-off of these dormant accounts together with the recommendation of the Local Chief Executive. Likewise, the Municipal Accountant have to issue certifications that the municipality has no more records or information, hence the chance to collect is no longer attainable.

We recommend the Municipal Assessor to prioritize the registration and transfer of title of the land to ensure ownership and to fully protect the interest of the Municipality in compliance with Section 39 of PD No.1445. Also, the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Assessor need to reconcile their records of the land owned by the LGU.

We recommend that management create a GAD Focal Point System to ascertain the alignment of GAD agenda and propose activities identified as gender issues. Also, we recommend the establishment of GAD database/sex-disaggregated data to come up with an evidenced-base programs, activities/projects intended for gender causes. Moreover, maximize the utilization of GAD funds in order to attain the objective for which the funds were provided.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to validate their records, reconcile any identified reconciling items and effect the necessary adjustments in order to present a reliable balance of the CIB-LCCA account in the financial statements.

We reiterated and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the Inventory Committee, Municipal Accountant, and MGSO to reconcile their respective records to provide accurate information of the PPE accounts.

We recommend that and the Management agreed that:
The Inventory Committee prepare and submit the CY 2023 RPCPPE immediately, and henceforth ensure the timely preparation and submission of the annual RPCPPE;
The Accounting Office and the Inventory Committee reconcile the PPE balances between the RPCPPE and the FS, and the Municipal Accountant present the correct balances in the FS; and
The Accounting Office immediately provide depreciation expenses for the aforementioned undepreciated PPEs using the estimated useful lives and straight-line method of depreciation, in accordance with COA Circular No. 2003-007.

We recommended and the Municipal Treasurer agreed to diligently monitor stale checks and communicate with payees for their timely encashment.

We also recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to make the necessary adjusting entries regarding the outstanding checks.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the concerned municipal officials/employees to immediately submit their liquidation reports and related documents or refund their cash advances.

We also recommended and the Accountable Officers agreed to ensure that all cash advances are liquidated within the specified period after the purpose of its grant has been served and/or before the end of each year.
We recommended and the Management agreed to stop the practice of charging to the LDRRMF expenses which are not enumerated in the aforesaid circular.

We recommended and the Management agreed to observe the provisions embodied in the Manual on Audit for Fuel Consumption of Government Vehicles.

We also recommended and the Management agreed to prepare and properly fill up the forms and reports prescribed in the manual.

We recommended and management agreed to prioritize and ensure the strict implementation of the “One-time cleansing of PPE accounts” in accordance with COA Circular No. 2020-006 by establishing an Inventory Committee comprising an adequate number of committed personnel tasked with spearheading its implementation, with the coordination of the Property Unit/Custodian and Municipal Accountant who shall provide with updated and reconciled balances of the PPE in their respective records.

We recommended that the management direct the accountable officers to follow the steps on the relief of accountability from properties pursuant to Sections 151 and 152 of COA Circular 92-386 and submit the request for relief of accountability to COA.
Shall the request be approved, drop the destroyed assets from the books of accounts by drawing the following journal entry:

Account Name | Account Code | Debit | Credit
Accumulated Depr. – Appropriate PPE Accounts |  | ₱1,960,459.57 | 
Government Equity | 3-01-01-010 | 3,601,996.18 | 
Appropriate PPE Accounts |  |  | ₱5,562,455.75

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant send demand letters to the concerned barangays, requesting the immediate submission of the liquidation reports for the funds transferred to them and make the necessary journal entries to take up the expenditures.
The Municipal Accountant shared that submission of liquidation documents by the barangay is usually delayed and that some barangays would wait for another funding from the Local Government Unit (LGU) before clearing their unliquidated fund transfers.
Delayed or non-implementation of the financial assistance at the agreed period of the project ultimately deprives the barangay constituents of the benefits that these projects would have provided.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant prepare adjusting journal entries for the aforementioned book reconciling items indicated in the BRS to ensure an accurate balance of the Cash in Bank account. To prevent the occurrence and accumulation of unadjusted book reconciling items in the future, we recommend the timely preparation and submission of monthly bank reconciliation statements.

We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to disclose in the Notes to Financial Statements the details of the balances of Unexpended LDRRMF as shown in Annex D of COA Circular No. 2012-002 for fair presentation and to enhance the understandability of the users of Financial Statements.

To enhance transparency and facilitate effective monitoring of the allocation of the LDRRMF, we recommended and the Municipal Accountant to implement sub-codes within the Special Accounts for this will provide a structured system to categorize and track the allocation of funds, promoting clearer financial reporting and facilitating better oversight of LDRRM Fund utilization.

We recommended that management refrain from granting additional funds to the barangays with unliquidated fund transfers. The municipal accountant shall provide the aforementioned certification only to implementing agencies with liquidated and properly accounted funds.
The Municipal Accountant, Municipal Budget Officer, and Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator discussed the LGU's provision of two types of counterpart funding to barangays: Women's Livelihood Programs and Barangay Infrastructure Projects. Consequently, there are situations where barangays can request additional fund transfers even without liquidating previous funds, since the granting of fund transfers by the management depends on their intended purpose.
The exception regarding the granting of additional fund transfers to barangays with previous unliquidated funds should be applicable to all types of counterpart funding, irrespective of their purpose.

We recommended and management agreed to comply the necessary forms, registry, and reports in accordance with COA Circular No. 2022-004, including the coordination of the Municipal Accountant and General Services Officer (GSO) to ensure that accountabilities over these properties are established.

We recommended the Municipal Mayor and Municipal Budget Officer to adhere with the mandates of RA No. 7160 and discontinue the practice of allowing MOOE items to be included in the continuing appropriations. Remaining balances classified as MOOE in the continuing appropriations as of December 31, 2023, shall be reverted to the unappropriated surplus of the general fund which shall thereafter be available for expenditure through subsequent enactment. Moreover, we recommended that projects funded out of the 20% development fund only include items that shall partake the nature of investment or capital expenditures in consonance to DILG-DBM JMC No. 2017-1 and 2020-1, dated February 22, 2017 and March 27, 2020, respectively.
The Municipal Budget Officer affirmed that succeeding classifications of budgets under the 20% Development Fund will be capital outlay instead of MOOE.

We recommended that the Municipal officials concerned prioritize and expedite the implementation of projects funded out of the 20% Development Fund for CY 2023. They shall also reassess and reconsider the feasibility of budgeted projects that are difficult to implement, allocating funds only to projects that are ready for procurement and implementation in the upcoming years.
The MPDC emphasized that majority of these projects are already on-going and that the LGU is trying its best to optimize the utilization of the fund.

We recommended and management ensured that the list of all ongoing government PPAs and those that are to be implemented for the ensuing year and the corresponding monthly/quarterly monitoring reports are submitted within the prescribed period in COA Circular No. 2013-004 dated January 30, 2013, as amended by COA Circular No. 2015-006 dated August 20, 2015.

We recommended that the Accounting and Treasury Office regularly coordinate regarding the identification and management of stale checks. To prevent the occurrence of stale checks in the future, the Municipal Treasurer shall issue written notices to the concerned payees at least one month before the checks become stale. Further, the Municipal Accountant shall make the necessary and timely adjustments in the books of accounts.

We recommended and management agreed to update their Monthly Report of Accountability for Accountable Forms (RAAF) and ensure to account all accountable forms with or without money value, including checks for their various bank accounts.
We recommended that subsequent cash advances be supported with a Physical and Financial Plan, whose total budget is equal to the amount of cash advance, serving as the basis or disbursements.

We recommended and the OIC-Municipal Accountant agreed to prepare journal entry vouchers to recognize the adjustment of the Construction in Progress account for completed projects to the appropriate PPE accounts. In addition, recognize the unrecorded depreciation to the Prior Period Adjustment account to present fairly the Government Equity account of the Municipality.

We recommended and the OIC-Municipal Accountant agreed to prepare adjusting journal entry to reflect the correct balance of the RPT and SET Receivables, and the Deferred RPT and SET Income pursuant to Section 20 of the NGAS Manual Volume 1 for LGUs for fair presentation of these accounts in the financial statements.

We recommended and the OIC-Municipal Accountant agreed to make the necessary adjusting journal entry to correct the PPE account balance, as follows:

under the PPE Account
We recommended and the Management agreed to consider the adoption of the revaluation model for Land account to present fairly the Land account in the financial statements.

We recommended and the OIC-Municipal Accountant agreed to prepare the necessary adjusting journal entry to re-classify the unexpended balances of QRF and DRRM-MOOE of the LDRRM Fund to Trust Liability-DRRMF account.

We recommended and Management agreed to instruct the Inventory Committee to conduct physical count of all supplies and materials inventory within the prescribed timeframe to facilitate the preparation of the Report of Physical Count of Inventories (RPCI) for every semester of a given year in accordance to COA Circular No. 2002-003 dated June 20, 2002.
We recommended and the Chairperson of the Bids and Awards Committee agreed to submit the lacking documents to fully established compliance with RA 9184, its IRR and related GPPB issuances.

We recommended and the Management agreed to direct the OIC-Municipal Accountant to submit the paid disbursement vouchers on the grant of cash advance for Confidential Fund and its required supporting documents as listed in the COA-DBM-DILG-GCG-DND Joint Circular No. 2015-01 to the COA Audit Team Leader within seven (7) days from release of check to verify the propriety and completeness of the required documents.

We recommended the following:

The Municipal Treasurer monitor checks issued and notify payees of the existence of the checks at least one month before checks become stale; and

The Municipal Accountant make necessary adjustments for stale checks by debiting, Cash in Bank, LCCA account and crediting Accounts Payable or Prior year’s adjustment, as the case maybe.

We recommended that the Municipality ensures that transactions related to SEF are supported with complete documentary requirements before making any payments in compliance with the existing laws, rules and regulation of SEF.

We recommended that the Municipality stop the practice of granting cash advance to its elected officials other than for their travelling expenses in compliance with COA Circular No. 97-002 dated February 10, 1997.

We commended the Management for its compliance on appropriation and utilization of the 20% Development Fund for eligible development projects in accordance with DBM-DOF-DILG JMC No. 1 dated November 4, 2020.

We recommended that the Management do the following:

Stop the charging of monthly wages of municipal job orders against the LDRRM Fund in compliance with Section 5 of NDRRMC, DBM and DILG Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-1 dated March 25, 2013 and NDRRMC, DILG, DBM and CSC Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2014-1 dated April 4, 2014.

Restore to the LDRRM Fund the amount representing disbursements for payment of wages of job orders in CY 2022 that were erroneously charged against the fund which shall be re-appropriated to finance only those programs, projects and activities identified to be funded under the 5% LDRRM Fund for the ensuing year.

We recommended that the Management abide with the new accounting policy on increasing the threshold of PPE to ₱50,000.00. Items with value less than ₱50,000.00 shall be reclassified as semi-expendable property under the inventory account in compliance with IPSAS 12, and instruct the Municipal Accountant to prepare the necessary Journal Entry Vouchers (JEVs) to take up the appropriate adjusting/reclassification entries in compliance with GAA for FY 2022 and COA Circular No. 2022-004.

We recommended that the Management call the attention of the Accounting Office to prepare adjusting entries to reclassify/record those fund transfers from Other Payables to Due to NGAs account in the Trust Fund books in compliance with COA Circular 2015-009 dated December 01, 2015.

We recommended and the Management agreed to:

Require the Municipal Accountant and the Property Custodian to maintain the PPELC and the PC, respectively, which are the bases in recording and monitoring the PPE;

Direct the Accounting Office and the Inventory Committee to reconcile the PPE balances between the RPCPPE and the FS, and the Municipal Accountant present the correct balances in the FS; and


Effect the one-time cleansing of PPE accounts in accordance with the guidelines and procedures prescribed under COA Circular No. 2020-006.

We recommended and Management agreed to:
Exhaust all efforts to determine the exact address of resigned/retired accountable personnel with unsettled cash advances and send demand letters requiring the immediate liquidation of their cash advances; and
Request for the write-off of unliquidated cash advances which have become dormant, if warranted, in accordance with COA Circular 2016-005.

We recommended and the Management agreed to provide additional personnel to assist in the preparation and submission of financial reports and supporting documents, so that financial reports and supporting documents are submitted within the required period.

We recommended and the Management agreed to:

Refrain/stop from using fund transfers subsidy from the GF-Proper to fund/pay the PS requirements/expenses of the LEE;

Cause the review and evaluation of the plantilla of personnel of the LEE and its operational expenses, particularly in personnel services, in relation to the revenues earned, to preclude the necessity of funds transfer/subsidy from GF to fund PS expenses; and

Consider and cause the implementation of the appropriate option provided in Section 3.4 of the aforementioned Local Budget Circular 100 when revenues of the LEE cannot fully fund its PS requirements.

We recommended and the Management agreed that the payments for supplies and materials, as well as other operating expenses of the Municipality, be made directly to the suppliers in compliance with the pertinent provisions of RA No. 9184.
We recommended and Management agreed to terminate the contracts of service for consultants and observe the provision of the R.A. 9184 and its IRR in procuring consulting services.

We recommended to direct all the Department Heads/employees concerned to submit their detailed action plans on the recommendations including that of prior years, and the information gathered therein to be used to properly accomplish the Agency Action Plan and Status of Implementation, which should then be submitted to COA.
We also recommended that henceforth, the LGU of Buenavista shall, within 60 days from receipt of the Annual Audit Report, submit to COA, either in printed form or by way of an electronic document, a status report on the actions taken on said audit observations and recommendations.

We recommended and Management agreed to:

Direct the MDRRMO to submit monthly to the Auditor the Report on Utilization of the DRRMF; and

Direct the Municipal Accountant to include a discussion on the details of the unexpended balances of the MDRRMF in the Notes to the Financial Statements as required in Annex D of COA Circular No. 2012-002.

We recommended and the Management agreed to:
Hasten the implementation and completion of the remaining 2022 non-implemented development projects so that the 20% Development Fund is optimally utilized in the attainment of the municipality’s socio-economic development goals; and

Extensively plan the projects that should be included in the Annual Development Plan/Annual Investment Program and ensure that the same is feasible for implementation within the timeline or as programmed.

We recommend that management require the Municipal Accountant to prepare the lapsing schedule and trace the items under the Road Networks account, reflect the unrecognized depreciation in the year of completion to bring the accumulated depreciation to its accurate balance. Effect an adjusting journal entry for the understated depreciation expense and accumulated depreciation as follows:

We recommend that the Municipal Accountant trace the entries made affecting these accounts and reconcile the differences by making the necessary adjusting entries. We also recommend that, henceforth, the Municipal Accountant ensure that all necessary entries are properly recorded in order to reflect the correct balances in the financial statements.

We recommend that the management trace the period the discounts on RPT/SET remained unrecognized and adjust the RPT/SET Receivable based on the computation. Prior year’s discounts should be taken up as adjustments both on the RPT/SET Receivable and Deferred RPT/SET accounts and current year’s discounts are to be recorded as a deduction from the total Real Property and Special Education Tax, as follows:

To recognize adjustments on the RPT/SET Receivables during the year CY 2022:


To recognize current year discounts during CY 2022:

We recommended that management ensure that at the end of each year, the Municipal Accountant transfer all the unexpended/unobligated balance of the QRF and the DRRMF - MOOE to the Special Trust Fund under the account “Trust Liability - DRRM”. Also, require the Municipal Accountant to transfer all unexpended QRF and LDRRMF-MOOE of qualified prior years to the Special Trust Fund under the account Trust Liabilities-DDRM with the following adjusting entry:

We recommend that management require the submission of Accomplishment Reports to support the payment of salaries and wages of these Job Order Personnel. We also recommend revising the Job Order Contract and including therein the job descriptions and/or statement of duties and responsibilities of the Job Order Personnel to ensure that services provided by the Job Order Personnel are not redundant or regular employees can do that.

We recommend that the management instruct the Municipal Accountant to reclassify the non-current portion of Loans Payable-Domestic for better presentation of liabilities of the LGU in the Statement of Financial Position as at year-end/reporting date. We also recommend that the Municipal Accountant provide detailed information on the composition of the Loans Payable-Domestic in the notes to financial statements.

We recommend that the management instruct the Municipal Accountant to reclassify the Loans Receivables-Others account as non-current asset for better presentation in the Statement of Financial Position as at year-end/reporting date. We also recommend that the Municipal Accountant provide detailed information on the composition of the Loans Receivables-Others in the notes to financial statements.

We recommend that management submit to the audit team an explanation on the inconsistencies of the construction period. Also, may we require a realistic construction period so as not to create doubt on its completion.

We recommend that management require the Municipal Accountant to see to it that claims for repair has to be supported with records on repair history as required under Section 9.1.3.4 of COA Circular No. 2012-001. Also, instruct the Accounting Office to reconstruct the maintenance/repair record of each vehicle owned and/or operated by the municipal government.

We recommend the Municipal Accountant to exercise prudence and diligence in the disbursement of funds and to strictly adhere to the provisions of DBM Circular No. 2007-3 on the payment of honoraria to BAC and TWG members. We also recommend the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Budget Officer to ensure that savings from the approved local budget has been approved by the Sanggunian before effecting payment chargeable to the General Fund.

We recommend the Municipal Agriculturist to account for its distribution and submit to the Audit Team the issuance/distribution list of the various farm supplies and duly signed acknowledgment receipts. Further, ensure that subsequent purchases of farm supplies intended for distribution are supported with a targeted list of recipients and the issuance/distribution list thereof.

We recommend that management should follow up the production of the SB Resolution requesting for the write-off of these dormant accounts together with the recommendation of the Local Chief Executive. Likewise, the Municipal Accountant have to issue certifications that the municipality has no more records or information, hence the chance to collect is no longer attainable.

We recommend that Management refrain from releasing more funds to these leagues unless the required official receipts of the government are issued by the recipient Leagues concerned proper accounting and uphold observance of accountability and transparency of all government transactions.

We recommended that:

The Municipal Treasurer, in coordination with the Municipal Assessor, to prepare and submit the certified list of delinquent real property accounts with its tax due to effect the necessary adjusting entries to the RPT and SET Receivables together with its Deferred RPT and SET Income accounts.

Likewise, that the Municipal Assessor submit the assessment roll to the Municipal Treasurer as basis by the Municipal Treasurer for the preparation of the certified list of tax due from real property taxpayers.

Moreover, that the Municipal Treasurer submit the certified list of real property tax due to the Municipal Accountant for setting up the RPT and SET Receivable accounts at January 1 of each year.

Finally, that the Municipal Accountant recognize the RPT and SET Receivable at the beginning of the year or January 1 of each year based on the certified list of real property tax due pursuant to Sections 19 and 20 of the NGAS Manual Volume 1 for fair presentation of these accounts in the financial statements.
