b. Cause the review and evaluation of the plantilla of personnel of the LEE and its operational expenses, particularly in personnel services, in relation to the revenues earned, to preclude the necessity of fund transfers/ subsidy from GF to fund PS expenses; and
c. Consider and cause the implementation of the appropriate option provided in Section 3.4 of the aforementioned Local Budget Circular 100 when revenues of the LEE cannot fully fund its PS requirements.

We recommended that the Management:
Instruct the Inventory Committee to coordinate with the General Services Office (GSO) in the preparation of a Physical Inventory Plan (PIP) in accordance to COA Circular No. 2020-006 to promote the conduct of the required annual physical count of PPE, and to address the unreconciled balances between the Accounting Office and GSO caused by non-existing/missing PPE without available record of accountability;
Require the GSO to coordinate with the Accounting Office to determine the items to be included in the RPCPPE considering the capitalization threshold of ₱15,000.00, and with the Inventory Committee on the details and other relevant information to ensure the proper accomplishment of the RPCPPE; and
Strictly observe the rules and regulations on the one-time cleansing of PPE accounts in accordance to COA Circular No. 2020-006.

We recommended that the Management:
Revisit the Municipality’s supply and materials management to promote proper custodianship over these inventory items, in accordance with COA Circular No. 92-386 dated October 20, 1992, prescribing Rules and Regulations on Supply and Property Management in the Local Governments; and
Direct the Inventory Committee to conduct physical count of all supplies and materials inventory within the prescribed timeframe to facilitate the preparation of the Report of Physical Count of Inventories (RPCI) for every semester of a given year in accordance to COA Circular No. 2002-003 dated June 20, 2002.

We recommended that the Management instruct the Municipal Health Officer to strictly observe the provisions of the BGCM that will effectively prevent and mitigate the transmission of the COVID 19 disease.

We recommended that the Management:
Instruct the Municipal Accountant fast-track the reconciliation of its accounts to the available records on file to determine the accurate ending balances on its financial statements;
The Municipal Accountant to provide subsidiary ledgers on its installed accounting system in detail to monitor the receipt and utilization of the funds received from various national government agencies for the implementation of various programs and/or projects;
Use an electronic accounting system which adhere to the current accounting standard such the electronic New Government Accounting System (e-NGAS); and
Observe the guidelines provided in COA Circular No. 2015-009 on the use of appropriate accounts under the PPSAS. Accordingly, prepare journal vouchers for the following entries:

Instruct the Municipal Accountant to fast-track the reconciling of records from its financial statements to its subsidiary ledgers, request training and/or assistance from the system developer to fully understand the accounting system installed, and familiarize the accounting standards set out in the PPSAS;
Consider the installation of e-NGAS as the agency’s electronic accounting system to prevent slow down and/or corruption of accounting records and to provide accurate presentation of its financial statement; and
Instruct the Municipal Accountant to prepare the following journal entries to recognized the completed projects to its appropriate asset accounts under the Trust Fund and General Fund books, to wit:

We recommended that the Management instruct the Municipal Treasurer to stop the practice of transferring the share of the barangays from the collections of real property tax, CTC and other receipts from the General Fund books to the Trust Fund books. Additionally, the Municipal Treasurer should remit this amounts to the respective barangay governments pursuant to the provisions of the Local Government Code.

We recommended that the Management:
Require those in-charge of the projects/program to submit the lacking documents as mentioned above; and
Direct the Municipal Accountant to thoroughly review the supporting documents in all transactions before certifying the completeness and propriety thereof.

We recommend that the Local Chief Executive, together with the Municipal Development Council ensure the optimum utilization of the 20% Development Fund by carefully identifying the priority programs, projects and activities that are implementation-ready, and would truly and immediately cater the needs of the constituents, alongside of it is the meticulous determination of their corresponding appropriation to avoid wastage of time and resources.

We recommended that the Management:
Instruct the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to stop requiring the barangay governments to submit a Barangay Council resolution before remitting their share from the collections of RPT, CTC, environmental fee and PCA; and
Observe the rules and regulations provided in the Local Government Code. Finally, release all the unremitted shares to the barangay governments pursuant to the provisions of the Local Government Code.

We recommended that the Management:
Require the Supplier to replace the items with noted deficiencies and follow description/s indicated in the Purchase Order;
Ensure that person with technical expertise is included as a member of the inspectorate team when procured goods has a technical description to make certain that the delivered items were in accordance with the description/s in the Purchase Order; and
Submit justification why the printers with the same description have different prices.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the:

a) Municipal GSO and Municipal Accountant to make a
reconciliation for the noted variance in the recorded PPE as against the
RPCPPE;

b) Creation of an Inventory Committee for the purpose of one-time cleansing of
PPE; and

c) Municipal GSO to prepare and submit the approved Physical Inventory Plan to
the Audit Team at least 10 days before the scheduled start of inventory-taking
activities in adopting COA Circular No. 2020-006 relative to the one-time
cleansing of PPE accounts.

Management’s Comment:
The Municipal Treasurer and GSO-Designate are in the process of reconciliation. It was
also mentioned that the one time- cleansing of the PPE of the Municipality could be
undertaken immediately, hence, the GSO designate is already planning the preparation
of the Physical Inventory Plan.

Auditor’s Rejoinder:
The Audit Team valued the work exerted by the Municipal Accountant and GSO
designate on the reconciliation of the PPE balances and their plans on the
implementation of the one-time cleansing of PPE. However, the team expected that the
inventory taking be done immediately so that the other procedures relative to one time
cleansing enumerated on COA Circular No. 2020-006 will be conducted.

We recommended that the Management direct the:
MAO to (i) constantly monitor the reconciling items identified in the BRS and make the necessary adjustments in the books; and (ii) restore to the CIB balance the total amount of the stale checks and unreleased checks by debiting the CIB – LCCA account and crediting the Payables account to reflect the correct balances of the affected accounts at year-end; and
MTO to (i) submit the list of all unreleased/unclaimed checks as of December 31 of each year to the MAO as basis in recording the restoration of its total amount to the cash in bank and the accounts payable balances at year-end and reversal of said entry in the ensuing year; (ii) issue/send written notices to the payees at least one month before it becomes stale; and (iii) ensure that all collections are deposited intact daily or not later than the next banking day in accordance with Section 32 of the MNGAS for LGUs.

We recommended that the Management instruct the Municipal Accountant to (a) conduct regular and periodic verification, analysis and validation of the existence and valuation of their receivables; (b) prepare the necessary adjusting entries to recognize impairment losses for receivables which has uncertainty of collectability or the amount which has ceased to be probable as required under IPSAS 29; (c) prepare aging of dormant receivables on a quarterly basis (Annex 1 of COA Circular No. 2016-005); and (d) request authority to write-off of the dormant accounts receivables which have been outstanding for ten or more years in accordance with COA Circular No. 2016-005.

We recommended the Municipal Accountant to (i) coordinate with the PAO to address the discrepancies in the reciprocal accounts, Due from LGUs and Due to LGUs and record the shares of barangays directly in their respective books; (ii) effect the necessary adjustments to reflect the correct amounts of receivable, payable and income accounts in the books at year-end; (iii) make representation with the Provincial Government for the immediate release/remittance of receivable amounts due to the Municipality.

We recommended that the Management direct the Municipal Accountant to: a) determine the causes of the discrepancy between the Due from Other Funds account and Due to Other Funds account and furnish the Audit Team with the related documents pertaining to the said transactions; b) draw the necessary adjusting entries to reflect the correct balances of the intra-agency receivables and payables; and c) prepare a reconciliation report of reciprocal accounts to ensure that all reconciling items are resolved and/or adjusted.

We recommended the Management to:
require the Municipal Accountant (i) adopt consistently the perpetual inventory method and recognize inventories as asset at the time of purchase, and expense upon consumption of the inventories in accordance with Section 114 and 121 of MNGAs for LGUs; (ii) make necessary adjustments for a) remaining supplies/inventories on hand as of year-end, if any and b) the overstatement in the Construction in Progress and understatement of the Construction Material Inventory account; and (iii) adjust the inventory accounts as reflected in the Financial Statements by effecting the following correcting entries:
require the Inventory Committee to (i) identify the remaining balance of the inventories amounting to ₱538,454.92 from CY 2020 Financial Statements remained on hand; and (ii) furnish their report to this office for verification and to the Municipal Accountant for adjustment in the books of the Municipality; and
observe IPSAS No. 1 in the recording and reporting of the inventories for the fair presentation of the accounts in the Financial Statements.

We recommended the Management to instruct the Municipal Accountant to:
effect the following adjusting entry to record the depreciation of the PPEs totalling ₱15,054,751.14 to bring the amounts of the said assets to their correct carrying values:
provide adjusting entry for the PPEs amounting to ₱70,725,043.52 to record their depreciation for the current and previous years; and
observe paragraph 71 of IPSAS No. 17 by providing the periodical depreciation for all depreciable assets.
We reiterated our recommendations for the Management to direct the Municipal Accountant to: a) reconcile the PPE Subsidiary Ledgers with its corresponding PPE Ledger Card upon reconciliation of the Subsidiary Ledger with the RPCPPE to identify which assets do not have ledger card in order to provide one and ensure that the same are properly and completely filled up; b) gradually trace back the vouchers of the PPEs with ledger cards but with incomplete details to supply the missing information; and c) maintain proper and complete records of its PPEs in their ledger card for proper asset monitoring and management.

We recommended that the Management:
require the Municipal Treasurer to (i) religiously submit to the MAO a duly certified list showing the name of taxpayers and the amount due and collectible for the year; (ii) continuously post Notice of Delinquency to publicly accessible and conspicuous places for wider dissemination; (iii) consider formulating/devising new and straight strategies and impose civil remedies and other sanctions guided by the Local Government Code in order to fully address the low collection rate; and (iv) make representation with the Provincial Treasurer for other legal actions to enforce collections of delinquent taxes as provided in the Local Government Code;
direct the Municipal Accountant to set-up the RPT/SET Receivables at the beginning of the year based on RPT Account Register/Taxpayer’s Index Cards supported by a duly certified list of taxpayers and the amounts due and collectible for the year; and
direct the Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Accountant to reconcile their records pertaining to balances of RPT/SET Receivables and prepare the necessary adjustments, if necessary.

We recommended that the Management require the Municipal Accountant to:
effect the following adjusting entries to correct the balances of the Due to BIR accounts and to set up the Due to Other Funds account in the books of TF and SEF:
provide details and nature of the percentage taxes amounting to ₱238,977.24 recorded as receivables and provide a copy of the vouchers to the Audit Team for further verification. Once identified that said receivable pertains to amount due to the government, facilitate the immediate remittance to the BIR in order to avoid further incurrence of penalties and surcharges.
We recommended the Management to instruct the (a) Municipal Treasurer to promptly file and remit the monthly withholding taxes due to the BIR in order to avoid the possible imposition of interest, surcharges and penalties; and  (b) Municipal Accountant to (i) make the necessary adjusting entry to correct the Due to BIR account recorded twice in the books amounting to ₱278,511.87; (ii) identify the details of the percentage tax amounting to ₱53,144.40 and facilitate its immediate remittance  by coordinating with Municipal Treasurer; and (iii) record any interest, surcharges and penalties that may be subsequently imposed by the BIR as a personal liability of the employee responsible for filing and payment of the taxes due thereof.
We recommended the Management to instruct the Municipal Accountant to: a) facilitate the refund from the officials/employees the deficient taxes totalling ₱150,583.18 and the immediate remittance of the same to avoid further imposition of interests and surcharges that may be imposed by the BIR; and b) provide the correct computation of withholding tax to be deducted and withheld on compensation in accordance with Sections 79 and 80 of the NIRC applying the latest rules and regulations on withholding taxes.

We recommended the Management to:
require the Municipal Treasurer to facilitate the transfer of funds from MDRRM Special Trust Fund to the GF amounting to ₱148,020.00;
instruct the Municipal Accountant to draw the following accounting entries to record the transfer of funds to GF:
direct the Municipal Budget Officer to submit a satisfactory explanation for certifying that the aforementioned projects have sufficient and available appropriation in the budget of the Municipality; and
require the MDRRM Officer to: i) prepare and submit a comprehensive MDRRMFIP consisting of all PPAs, both current and continuing, from the General Fund and Special Trust Fund and consistently monitor the expenditures charged against each appropriation to avoid misappropriation of funds; and ii) ensure that the funds for the PPAs in the MDRRMFIP will not be utilized to other projects aside from its intended purpose in compliance with the rules and regulations for the allocation and utilization of the MDRRM Fund as prescribed under Sections 5.1.2 and 5.1.3 of COA Circular No. 2012-002.

We reiterated our recommendation that the Management: a) require the MDC through the MPDC to reassess the viability of their projects especially those that have remained unimplemented for almost three years and facilitate the realignment of those that cannot be feasibly accomplished and ensure its prompt implementation; b) instruct the MPDC and the Municipal Engineer to ensure that the projects planned in their AIP be carried out and completed during the year; and c) direct the MPDC and the Municipal Engineer to ensure that the Program of Works of each project is strictly followed as planned to prevent expenditures exceeding its appropriation and to avoid depletion of funds allocated for other PPAs.

We reiterated our recommendation that the Management require the: a) MPDO to spearhead the setting up and maintenance of GAD database with adequate and systematically gathered sex-disaggregated data which will provide the bases for gender analysis, planning, and policy formulation; and b) GFPS or TWG to provide the necessary technical assistance to the MPDO in ensuring that the GAD database is operational.
We recommended that the Management instruct the GAD Focal Person together with the concerned responsible offices identified in the GPB to maximize the utilization of GAD funds through the implementation of GAD-related PPAs addressing the persisting gender issues in the locality.
We recommended that the Management instruct the GFPS TWG to strictly comply with provisions of R.A. No. 9710 and its IRR in the formulation of the GPB to ensure that the GAD PPAs are all supportive or responsive to the gender issues of the locality.

We recommended that the Management direct: a) the Municipal Treasurer to cause the reversion to National Treasury the total amount of ₱189,664.90 unexpended balance of the BGCM pursuant to Section 5.0 of DBM Circular No. 2021-13 dated December 31, 2021; and b) the Municipal Accountant to draw the corresponding adjusting entries for the reversion of the BGCM Fund.

We reiterated our recommendations that the Management:
take the necessary actions to expedite the collection of the unsettled disallowances amounting to ₱8,388,417.67;
cause all the liable and certifying approving officers, who took part in the disbursement of disallowed expenditures, to assume responsibility in the final settlement of the remaining balance of the disallowance; and
require the Municipal Accountant to (i) record the excess payment of ₱2,048.87 from the cash shortage as other income by debiting Receivables - Disallowances/Charges account and crediting Miscellaneous Income account; (ii) ensure that before allowing settlement of disallowances through instalment payment the same was authorized and approved by COA; and (ii) submit to the Office of the Auditor a schedule containing partial settlement of disallowances of each liable persons and the corresponding Journal Entry Voucher for the issuance of notice of settlement.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the Planning and implementing Department to:
To conduct regular monitoring and evaluation of the 20% Development Fund projects in order to achieve desirable socio-economic development and environmental outcomes for the benefit of the intended beneficiaries and provide such information to the Office of the Mayor; and
To provide a regular status update to the Planning Department to avoid delayed on the implementation of programs, projects and activities.

We recommended that the LGU GFPS Executive Committee and the Technical Working Group ensure that all programs, projects and activities included in the Annual Gender and Development Plan and Budget be judiciously monitored and implemented so that the identified gender issues for the calendar year will be addressed pursuant to PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC No. 2013-01.

We recommended that the Municipal School Board formulate the school board budget strictly in accordance with the provisions of RA 7160 and DepEd – DBM – DILG Joint Circular No. 1, s. 2017 as amended by DepEd-DBM-DILG Joint Memorandum Circular (JMC) No. 1 s. 2020 dated August 27, 2020 and its implementing regulations in the utilization of the SEF. Only items among the required and priority projects enumerated in the regulations be allowed to be funded out of SEF. Responsible officials cease and desist charging expenditures of the office of the district supervisor from the school board budget.

We reiterated the following:
The Local Chief Executive require the concerned officers and employees who are currently employed with the LGU to liquidate their outstanding cash advances immediately thru submission of liquidation report with supporting documents or pay the amount of the unliquidated cash advance.  Likewise, exert extra effort to locate and resend demand letters to those who are no longer connected with the LGU; and
The Municipal Accountant withhold the salaries of concerned personnel who are currently employed with the LGU who failed to comply with the instructions on liquidation of their long outstanding cash advances as provided under COA Circular No. 97-002.

We recommended that the Municipal Engineering Department strictly follow the provision of COA Circular No. 2013-004 dated January 30, 2013. Close monitoring and supervision should be diligently performed by the official and staff to achieve the objective of transparency for each implemented projects.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive being the Municipal Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Chairman direct the MDRRMO and other concerned officials to maximize the utilization of 70 percent Mitigation Fund by giving preference to the implementation of disaster related programs, projects and activities as planned in order to address any adverse effect of disaster in compliance with NDRRMC-DBM-DILG Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-1 dated March 25, 2013.

We recommended that the management exert effort to determine whether these unused funds need to be returned to Source Agencies. If found that the funds need to be liquidated, prepare a report to liquidate the unused funds to Source Agencies in compliance with COA Circular No. 94-013 dated December 13, 1994.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive coordinate with the Source Agency for possible modification of the MOA in order to pursue with the implementation of the projects. Otherwise, revert the fund to the Source Agencies in compliance with the approved Memorandum of Agreement.

We recommended and Management agreed to:
Cause the preparation of the Inventory and Inspection Report for Unserviceable Property (IIRUP) by the Property Officer/Unit to facilitate the disposal of unserviceable properties;
Direct the Disposal Committee to expedite the disposal of unserviceable properties following the procedures set forth in COA Circular No. 92-386, to prevent further deterioration and to realize income therefrom if found to be saleable; and
Assess the recoverable service amount of unserviceable properties in accordance with the guidelines in IPSAS 17 and 21, and record the appropriate derecognition or impairment costs, to fairly present the asset’s carrying value in the financial statements.

We recommended and Management agreed to:
Direct the Inventory Committee and the Municipal Accountant to reconcile the Other Structures and Medical Equipment PPE accounts and ensure that PPEs included in the RPCPPE are duly recorded in the Property Cards and PPE Ledger Cards; and
Henceforth, physical count of PPE and reconciliation and adjustment of PPE account balances be conducted in accordance with the guidelines and procedures prescribed under COA Circular No. 2020-006, in order to avail of the one-time cleansing of PPE accounts.

We recommended and Management agreed to:
Issue demand letters or notices to the AOs with long outstanding unliquidated cash advances, and if warranted, cause the withholding of their salaries pursuant to Section 5.9 of COA Circular 97-002;
Exhaust all efforts to determine the exact address of resigned/retired accountable personnel with unsettled cash advances and send demand letters requiring the immediate liquidation of their cash advances; and
Request for the write-off of unliquidated cash advances which has become dormant, if warranted, in accordance with COA Circular No. 2016-005.

We recommended and Management agreed to direct the Municipal Accountant to formulate strategies as to work schedule and time management of concerned personnel in charge of the preparation and submission of financial reports and supporting documents.
We also recommended and the Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Accountant agreed to request actual printouts or snapshots of transactions from the depository bank, whenever necessary, so that preparation and submission of financial reports are within the reglementary period.

We recommended and Management agreed to direct the Local Development Council and concerned offices/departments/officials to:
Hasten the implementation and completion of the remaining 2021 non-implemented development projects so that the 20 percent Development Fund is optimally utilized in the attainment of the Municipality’s socio-economic development goals;
Immediately plan/prepare specific development programs/projects for the unprogrammed appropriations amounting to P234,430.00 and ensure timely implementation and completion of the projects; and
Extensively plan the projects that should be included in the Annual Development Plan/Annual Investment Program and ensure that the same are feasible for implementation within the timeline or as programmed.

We recommended and Management agreed to direct the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to submit all aforementioned lacking documents to the COA Audit Team and subsequently, ensure that complete supporting documents for disbursements are submitted before payment, in accordance with the existing accounting and auditing rules and regulations.

We recommend that management require the Municipal Accountant to prepare adjusting entries reclassifying the Work/Zoo Animals to Breeding Stocks and for future transactions, record it to its appropriate account as provided in COA Circular No. 2015-009 in order to present fairly the financial statements.

We recommend that the management instruct the Municipal Accountant to reclassify the non-current portion of Loans Payable-Domestic for better presentation of liabilities of the LGU in the Statement of Financial Position as at year-end/reporting date. We also recommend that the Municipal Accountant provide detailed information on the composition of the Loans Payable-Domestic in the notes to financial statements.

We recommend that the management instruct the Municipal Accountant to reclassify the Loans Receivables-Others account as non-current asset for better presentation in the Statement of Financial Position as at year-end/reporting date. We also recommend that the Municipal Accountant provide detailed information on the composition of the Loans Receivables-Others in the notes to financial statements.

We recommend that management submit to the audit team an explanation on the inconsistencies of the construction period. Also, may we require a realistic construction period so as not to create doubt on its completion.

We recommend that Management observe and comply the necessary requirements prior to the disposal of its properties as required under COA Circular No. 92-386. Also, submit the necessary documentary requirements as well as justification on the non-compliance on the procedure on disposal of properties.

We recommend that management require the Municipal Accountant to record the excess collections on the sale of bid documents at year-end as income to present fairly the balances in the financial statements of the LGU.

We recommend that management require the Municipal Accountant to see to it that claims for repair has to be supported with records on repair history as required under Section 9.1.3.4 of COA Circular No. 2012-001. Also, instruct the Accounting Office to reconstruct the maintenance/repair record of each vehicle owned and/or operated by the municipal government.

We recommend the Municipal Accountant to exercise prudence and diligence in the disbursement of funds and to strictly adhere to the provisions of DBM Circular No. 2007-3 on the payment of honoraria to BAC and TWG members. We also recommend the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Budget Officer to ensure that savings from the approved local budget has been approved by the Sanggunian before effecting payment chargeable to the General Fund.

We recommend that Management stop the practice of charging prior year's BAC and TWG members honoraria to current year’s collection and that proper matching of expenses against appropriation has to be observed pursuant to Section 119 of PD No. 1445. Likewise, officials who continue to authorize the payment of such expenditures are jointly and severally liable for any disallowances.

We recommend that management require the Municipal Engineer to submit to the audit team the Program of Work for the project Rehabilitation and Improvement of Municipal Waterworks System (Salintubig Program) Design and Build for review and evaluation. Also, submit an explanation/clarification on the inconsistencies of the major work item quantities and cost between the ABC, Contract and SWA.

We recommend that management stop the practice of granting/approving work suspension and contract time extensions for reasons other than what is enumerated in RA 9184. Also require the Municipal Engineer to submit explanation/justification as to why the request for suspension and contract time extension for Suspension Order No.1 and 2 were granted. Otherwise, liquidated damages has to be collected from the contractor.

We recommend the Municipal Agriculturist to account for its distribution and submit to the Audit Team the issuance/distribution list of the various farm supplies and duly signed acknowledgement receipts. Further, ensure that subsequent purchases of farm supplies intended for distribution are supported with a targeted list of recipients and the issuance/distribution list thereof.

We recommend that the Municipal Accountant adhere to the provisions prescribed in the 2016 Revised Implementing Rules and Regulations of RA 9184 pertaining to warranty security, not only in the infrastructure projects but also for the procurement of goods. Henceforth, ensure that future procurements be deducted with the 1% retention money or require a special bank guarantee of the same amount.

We recommend that management stop the practice of paying excess employers share on the monthly premium contributions to HDMF pursuant to Section 7 of RA 9679. Further, effect the refund of the government share of the CY 2021 and previous years’ excessive contributions of employers share to fund.

We recommend that management stop utilizing the 5% LDRRMF for expenses other than those covered under COA Circular No.2012-002 and DILG Memorandum Circular No.2021-73. Also require the Municipal Budget Officer to closely monitor and ensure that the LDRRM planned Programs/Projects/Activities are implemented and give due regard on the maximum utilization of the 70% for pre disaster preparedness programs so that the intended purpose is attained/achieved.
We recommend that management require the Municipal Budget Officer to submit the SAAOB on the 20th day of the following month to facilitate the verification of transactions and reports of the LGU.

We recommended that the Management:
a.	Prepare the monthly BGCM Utilization Reports on time and post them in three conspicuous places. After which, send a written notice of the posting of reports to the other oversight bodies as provided in Item 4.4 of the DBM LBC No. 125.
b.	Also, furnish the Office of the Auditor copy of the said written notice.
observations.managementAction: The Management have already submitted BGCM Reports to the Auditor.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Management:
a.	Stop the charging to BGCM Fund the fuel used for purposes that are not in accordance with Item 3.6 of DBM Local Budget Circular No. 125. Instead, spend the amounts to PPAs that can help strengthen the prevention and mitigation measures.
b.	Adhere strictly to the provisions of DBM Local Budget Circular No. 125 in the utilization of the BGCM Fund of the Municipality.
observations.managementAction: The Management have already refunded the said amount to the BGCM account.
The Management have already submitted the lacking documents except for the computation on the hazard pay that should have been received by the Job Orders/Contract of Service Workers.

The Management have already posted the procurements at the GPPB Online Portal and have submitted the lacking documents; likewise, they agreed to comply with the applicable laws, rules and regulations on the submission of complete supporting documents.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Management:
a.	Submit justification/s for the failure of the LGU to comply with the documents required as stipulated in the Memorandum of Agreement signed between the Municipality and DSWD on April 3, 2020.
b.	Moving forward, ensure strict compliance with the roles and responsibilities required for the LGU in accordance with the stipulations set forth in the Memorandum of Agreement signed between the Municipality and other agencies.
observations.managementAction: The Management have already submitted the lacking documents and agreed to strictly comply with the stipulations set in the MOA with other agencies.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the  Management:
a. Create an Inventory Committee that is composed of two or more employees including the GSO who will conduct physical count of all PPEs which shall be the basis in preparing the RPCPPE.
b. Direct the Accountant to compute depreciation expense for the Flood Control Systems, Water Supply Systems, and Markets accounts.
c. Direct the GSO to provide complete information in the RPCPPE, properly classify the PPEs according to their type and assign Property Numbers to all PPEs for easy identification especially during inventory-taking to determine non-existing/missing PPEs.
d. Refer to COA Circular No. 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020 on the specific guidelines and procedures to assist the LGU in coming up with reliable PPE balances that are verifiable as to existence, condition and accountability.
e. Direct the Accountant to disclose in the Notes to Financial Statements, the PPEs with their beginning balances found in the accounting system which could not be traced.
observations.managementAction: The General Services Office and the Accounting Office are still in the process of reconciling the Property, Plant and Equipment accounts. An Inventory Committee was already created by virtue of Executive Order No. 03 series of 2021 issued by the former Municipal Mayor on January 13, 2021

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Management:
a.	Create an Inventory Committee that is composed of two or more employees including the GSO who will conduct physical count of all inventories which shall be the basis in preparing the RPCI.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended the Management to require the Municipal Accountant to: 


a)	effect immediately the transfer of the adjustment of: i) unexpended balance of CY 2020 30% QRF and 70% MF- MOOE of the LDRRMF; and ii) 70% MF- CO of CY 2019 to the General Fund taking into consideration the completed projects and reprogrammed projects for CY 2020; 

b)	retain the 70% MF-CO for CY 2020 in the General Fund in compliance with Section 5.1.11 of COA Circular No. 2012-002 ;

c)	separate in the Report on Utilization of DRRMF and in the Notes to Financial Statements the 30% QRF and the 70% MF-MOOE in the Special Trust Fund and include a separate line item for the Continuing Appropriation of the General Fund for the Capital Outlay in order to clearly present the LDRRM Fund in the report on utilization and in the Notes to Financial Statements; and

d)	present separately in the Report on Utilization of DRRMF the utilization from the General Fund and the Special Trust Fund for better monitoring of the LDRRM Fund.




We recommended the management to: a) require the LDRRM Council to ensure that the programs, projects, and activities outlined in their LDRRMF Investment Plan addresses the current disaster vulnerabilities of the Municipality leaning most of their funds and effort to the containment of COVID-19 and that such plans were timely and fully implemented specifically those that are allocated out of the 70% Mitigation Fund; b) convene with the MDRRM Council to review and evaluate planned PPAs to ensure that identified PPAs are viable for implementation and develop strategies to identify and  address the delay of implementation; and c) consistently utilize the LDRRM Fund efficiently and effectively for the achievement of the goals and objectives of disaster risk reduction and enhanced disaster preparedness as set in the Republic Act No. 10121.

The Audit Team recommended the Management to require the MDRRMO to:

a)	submit a comprehensive and integrated LDRRMFIP incorporating those PPAs charged against the LDRRMF in the General Fund that are reprogrammed/realigned as well as those that are charged to the unexpended balance of the LDRRMF in the Special Trust Fund under a separate caption in compliance to COA Circular No. 2012-002 and COA Circular No. 2013-1; and

b)	consistently comply with the accounting and reporting rules and regulations regarding allocation and utilization of the LDRRMF as stated in the COA Circular No. 2012-002 dated September 12, 2012 and COA Circular No. 2013-1 dated March 25, 2013 to avoid misuse and misappropriation.

We recommended and the Management agreed to (i) appropriate and utilize solely the LDRRMF to programs, projects and activities that are supportive of the disaster risk management activities as provided under COA Circular No. 2012-002 and Section 5.0 of NDRRMC-DBM-DILG JMC No. 2013-01; and (ii) provide appropriation for LDRRM office from General Fund proper necessary in the conduct of its day to day operations.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended to instruct the Accounting Office to take actions on the following: 

Exert effort to analyze the account and immediately prepare an adjusting entries to recognize in the books unrecorded audit disallowances on the basis of NFD, if any, and drop in the books of accounts unrecorded settlement thereof under the NSSDC to obtain fair presentation of the account in the financial statements;

Always ensure that audit disallowances that have become final and executory, on the basis of the NFD are recorded in the books of accounts and settlement thereof under the NSSDC are dropped therefrom; and

Maintain an updated subsidiary ledger/records for each official/employee determined to be liable for the amount disallowed to ensure that audit disallowances including their settlements, are properly monitored and reconciled with the SASDC issued by the Auditor.

We recommended to instruct the Accounting Office to take actions on the following: 

Exert effort to analyze the account and immediately prepare an adjusting entries to recognize in the books unrecorded audit disallowances on the basis of NFD, if any, and drop in the books of accounts unrecorded settlement thereof under the NSSDC to obtain fair presentation of the account in the financial statements;

Always ensure that audit disallowances that have become final and executory, on the basis of the NFD are recorded in the books of accounts and settlement thereof under the NSSDC are dropped therefrom; and

Maintain an updated subsidiary ledger/records for each official/employee determined to be liable for the amount disallowed to ensure that audit disallowances including their settlements, are properly monitored and reconciled with the SASDC issued by the Auditor.

We recommended that:

a.	The Municipal Accountant shall conduct verification, analysis and validation of the existence of the long unliquidated cash advances, and prepare the necessary adjusting entries for the recognition of the impairment in accordance with the Philippine Public Sector Accounting Standards (PPSAS).

b.	The Municipal Mayor shall file the request for authority to write-off unliquidated cash advances to the COA Audit Team Leader (ATL) and/or Supervising Auditor (SA) on the following accountable officers/employees in compliance with COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016 with the following requirements:

I.	For the deceased accountable officers/employees:

i.	Schedule of dormant accounts by accountable officer/debtor/government entity and by account,  certified by the accountant and approved by the Head of the government entity;
ii.	Death Certificate issued by Philippine Statistics Authority (formerly National Statistics Office)

II.	For accountable officers/employees that are no longer connected with the LGU with unknown whereabouts:

i.	Schedule of dormant accounts  by accountable officer/debtor/government entity and by account,  certified by the accountant and approved by the Head of the government entity;
ii.	Certificate of no residence in the barangay of the  municipality/city of last known address;
iii.	Certification by Legal Officer of  the entity of no pending case relative to the account; and 
iv.	Certification by the responsible officials of the entity to the effect that there are no records/documents available to validate claim.
observations.reason:
Adjusting Entries were not prepared and submitted.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended the following:

Waste materials and unserviceable properties should be properly disposed according to applicable rules and regulations on supply and property management in LGUs.

Inventory and Inspection Report of Unserviceable Property (IIRUP) and Waste Materials Reports (WMR) should be diligently prepared, copies of which should be submitted to the Office of the Auditor.

We recommended that the Municipal Social Welfare and Development Officer, who took charge of relief distribution and inventory, fully account the relief goods purchased and distributed by submitting the duly acknowledged distribution lists. Management also submit an explanation why relief packs distributed were insufficient compared to the number of families evacuated despite availability of relief items, and why many were left after the relief operations. Proper planning and execution of relief operations should be instituted to avoid too much surplus of relief goods.

We recommended that management require the concerned barangays and the Liga to submit fund utilization reports in accordance with the provisions of the MOA. We recommend that the Municipal Treasurer draw checks in the name of the agency or its head or treasurer. Management should likewise conduct proper planning, budgeting and coordination to avoid delays and other problems that may arise in the execution of its programs, projects and activities.
observations.reason: FUR not submitted to the office of the auditor

observations.recommendation:
We recommended and the Municipal Accountant and the Property Officer agreed to:
a. Exhaust all efforts to determine the details of the accounts Other Land Improvements and School Buildings so that the Inventory Committee will be able to include the same in the physical count; and
b. Ensure that PPEs included in the RPCPPE are duly recorded in the Property Cards maintained by the Property Officer and PPE Ledger Cards of the Accountant, and see to it that both records are reconciled.

We recommended and Management agreed to:
a. Issue demand letters or notices to the AOs with long outstanding unliquidated cash advances, and if warranted, cause the withholding of their salaries pursuant to Section 5.9 of COA Circular 97-002;
b. Exhaust all efforts to determine the exact address of resigned/ retired accountable personnel with unsettled cash advances and send demand letters requiring the immediate liquidation of their cash advances; and
c. Request for the write-off of unliquidated cash advances of deceased AOs, if warranted, in accordance with COA Circular No. 2016-005.

We recommended and Management agreed to provide additional personnel to assist in the preparation and submission of financial reports and supporting documents, so that financial reports and its supporting documents are submitted within the required period.

We recommended and Management agreed the following:
a. Require the Municipal Accountant to:
i. Take up in the appropriate inventory account the cost of supplies, materials and relief goods procured for distribution, and in the corresponding expense account the subsequent issuance/distribution duly supported by the RIS and RSMI;
ii. Record in the books the inventory of welfare goods for distribution in custody of the Municipality based on the inventory report prepared; and
iii. Prepare and maintain Supplies Ledger Card for each inventory of welfare/ relief goods procured by the Municipality.
b. Require the Supply/Property Officer to:
i. Coordinate with the LDRRM Officer to prepare and submit an Inventory List/ Report of Welfare Goods for Distribution in custody of the Municipality as of December 31, 2020 in the prescribed format in Annex E; and
ii. Prepare and maintain Stock Cards, Requisition and Issue Slip, and Report of Supplies and Materials Issued for welfare goods.
c. Cause the submission of the duly validated/ approved list of beneficiaries, proof of receipt by and distribution to individual beneficiaries/ recipients, and Inventory List/Report of Welfare Goods for Distribution.

We recommended and Management agreed to:
a. Cause the inclusion of a warranty clause in succeeding Contracts/ POs under Emergency Procurement of the Bayanihan Act, to ensure assurance of replacement of/ recovery on goods delivered with defects, and/ or do not conform to quality standards and technical specifications of the contract; and
b. Ensure that all Contracts/POs contain complete detailed item specifications pursuant to COA Circular No. 96-010 dated August 15, 1996, to protect the interest of the government.

We recommended and Management agreed to:
a. The BAC to exert effort to access the GPPB Online Portal and comply with the posting requirements of all projects under the Bayanihan Act undertaken through Negotiated Procurement – Emergency Cases; and
b. The BAC to coordinate with the GPPB Technical Support Office so that problems in accessing the Online Portal are resolved.
We recommend that management review the operations of the Waterworks System. Consider increasing the water rates and other applicable fees and charges.
observations.managementAction:
Waterworks Supervisor to check the accuracy and the reliability of the billing system and ensure that the meter matches the electric bill.

Conduct periodic and continuous monitoring and review of the implementation of strategies with the end view that the desired results are achieved.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Treasurer and
Municipal Accountant to reconcile their records at least every end of the quarter to
ensure the reliability of cash accounts balances.

We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor require the:
• Municipal Accountant to file the request for write-off with the Commission on
Audit on the unaccounted receivables and those under the Carabao
Dispersal program; and
• Municipal Treasurer to exert effort in collecting receivables from recipients of
Self Employed Assistance loans and send demand letters.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant to
reconcile his ledger cards with the property cards maintained by the GSO-
Designate and make the necessary adjustments after reconciliation.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the General Services Officer-
Designate to submit the IIRUP with complete documentation based on the results of
the physical inventory in order to facilitate the disposal of unserviceable properties in
accordance with applicable rules and regulations.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to
remind the contractors to submit the documents pertaining to their claims. If they are
unable to support their claims with the necessary documents, revert the account to
the Unappropriated Surplus of the General Fund, subject to an authority from the
Sangguniang Bayan through a Resolution with a condition that appropriation shall be
made in the event that claim arises in the future pursuant to the provisions of Section
98 of PD No. 1445.

We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal
Engineer to submit the:
• list of all on-going government PPAs and those that are to be implemented
during the year; and
• monthly monitoring report in compliance with the existing regulation.

We recommended the Management to: a) require the Inventory Committee to conduct a physical count of all the reported PPEs owned and maintained by the Municipality and thereafter prepare and submit the RPCPPE within the prescribed period; b) compel the Municipal Treasurer’s Office (MTO) to reconcile the records   with  the Municipal Accounting Office in accordance with Section 124 of MNGAS for LGUs, Volume I, and make appropriate actions for any discrepancies noted during reconciliation; and c) instruct the Municipal Accounting Office to prepare adjusting entry for the ₱9,900,058.95 to the Road Networks account instead of the CIP Infrastructures Assets account to present their correct balances in the Financial Statements as prescribed in IPSAS No. 1.

oblige the Municipal Accountant to submit complete Bank Reconciliation Statements of all the bank accounts maintained by the Municipality for prompt verification as prescribed in Sections 3.1 to 3.4 of COA Circular No. 96-011 and Section 74 of the PD No. 1445; and
compel the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to book up all the necessary adjustments as identified in their Bank Reconciliation Statements to bring their cash accounts to its correct balances.

a) require the Municipal Accountant to report the stale checks as cancelled in their List of Unreleased Checks to be attached to their Report of Checks Issued and b) prepare the Journal Entry Voucher to take up the cancellation of the stale checks in order to reflect the correct balance of the Cash accounts.

The Audit Team recommended the Management to require the: a) Inventory Committee to conduct physical inventory count of the supplies and materials and submit the RPCI to this office and furnish the same to the Municipal Accounting Office for reconciliation; b) Municipal Accountant to adjust the Welfare Goods for Distribution Inventory Account to reflect the ending balance of ₱141,124.95 as per count of MSWDO; and c) Municipal Accountant to adopt Perpetual Inventory Method in recording inventories as prescribed under Section 114 of MNGAS to present fairly the actual inventory count in its Financial Statements.

We reiterated our recommendation that the Management instruct the Municipal Accountant to:
provide allowance for impairment for the receivables account which remained dormant for more than ten years to present fairly their receivables account as prescribed in Paragraph No. 67 of IPSAS No. 29; and
determine which of the dormant receivables is subject for impairment and those that can be written-off based on the allowable conditions for write-off enumerated in Section 7.4 of COA Circular 2016-005.

We reiterated our previous year’s recommendation that the Management:
require the Municipal Treasurer to (i) expedite the process of data cleansing of their records in order to proceed with the conduct of a more rigid tax campaign to increase the collection of real property tax; and (ii) continuously post Notice of Delinquency to publicly accessible and conspicuous place such as entrance of Municipal Building, Markets and the likes, for a wider dissemination of the notice;
designate collectors during the conduct of tax campaign in barangays; and
take into consideration the enforcement of the remedies for collection of real property tax as provided under the code which can either be administrative or judicial action.

We reiterated our recommendations that the Management require the Municipal Accountant to immediately draw a journal entries taking up the closure of depository accounts maintained with PNB and transfer its balances to LBP accounts in accordance with IPSAS No. 1 to present the accurate balances of the said bank accounts.

We recommended that the Management ensure to undertake and implement Covid-19 response and recovery interventions provided in Section 4 of RA No. 11494 and that additional allowable PS budget be utilized as intended to support the intention of the national government in addressing the pandemic.

We recommended the Management to direct the Municipal Accountant to: a) immediately reconcile the discrepancy amounting to ₱166,195.00 in the recorded balance of the BGCM and to draw the adjusting entry to correct the account; b) transfer the remaining balance of the BGCM to Subsidy from National Government (4-03-01-010) in accordance with Section 3.7 of DBM LBC No. 125 dated April 7, 2020; and c) create and maintain a special account in the general fund as provided in Section 3.7 of DBM LBC No. 125 and Section 107 of MNGAS.

We recommended the Management to direct the Municipal Planning Development Coordinator (MPDC) to: a) limit the charging of expenses out of BGCM fund to those that are COVID-19 related programs, projects and activities only; and b) submit a written explanation why the abovementioned expenditures were charged against the BGCM fund.

We recommended the Management to: a) require the BAC and Municipal Accountant to: i) submit a satisfactory explanation in the awarding of contracts and processing of disbursement vouchers, respectively, despite incomplete and/or non-submission of the required documentary requirements; and ii) ensure that all procurements were supplied with complete documentation before awarding of contract and before certifying and processing disbursement vouchers as prescribed in  Section 4 (6) of PD No. 1445 and Section 9.2 of COA Circular No. 2012-001; and b) oblige the Municipal Accountant to submit the necessary documentary requirements for all the procurements noted above.

We recommended that the Management: a) ensure the preparation using the prescribed format in Annex C and posting of the accumulated Monthly Report on Fund Utilization and Status of PPAs Implementation to three conspicuous places as required under Sections 4.2 and 4.3 of LBC No. 125; and b) send written notice to DBM, House of Representatives, Senate of the Philippines, House Committee on Appropriations, Senate Committee on Finance, and other offices upon posting of the reports as required under Section 4.4 of LBC No. 125.

We recommended the Management to require the Municipal Accountant to:
effect immediately the transfer of the adjustment of: i) unexpended balance of CY 2020 30% QRF and 70% MF- MOOE of the LDRRMF; and ii) 70% MF- CO of CY 2019 to the General Fund taking into consideration the completed projects and reprogrammed projects for CY 2020;
retain the 70% MF-CO for CY 2020 in the General Fund in compliance with Section 5.1.11 of COA Circular No. 2012-002 ;
separate in the Report on Utilization of DRRMF and in the Notes to Financial Statements the 30% QRF and the 70% MF-MOOE in the Special Trust Fund and include a separate line item for the Continuing Appropriation of the General Fund for the Capital Outlay in order to clearly present the LDRRM Fund in the report on utilization and in the Notes to Financial Statements; and
present separately in the Report on Utilization of DRRMF the utilization from the General Fund and the Special Trust Fund for better monitoring of the LDRRM Fund.

We recommended the management to: a) require the LDRRM Council to ensure that the programs, projects, and activities outlined in their LDRRMF Investment Plan addresses the current disaster vulnerabilities of the Municipality leaning most of their funds and effort to the containment of COVID-19 and that such plans were timely and fully implemented specifically those that are allocated out of the 70% Mitigation Fund; and b) consistently utilize the LDRRM Fund efficiently and effectively for the achievement of the goals and objectives of disaster risk reduction and enhanced disaster preparedness as set in the Republic Act No. 10121.

We recommended the Management to require the MDRRMO to:
submit a comprehensive and integrated LDRRMFIP incorporating those PPAs charged against the LDRRMF in the General Fund that are reprogrammed/realigned as well as those that are charged to the unexpended balance of the LDRRMF in the Special Trust Fund under a separate caption in compliance to COA Circular No. 2012-002 and COA Circular No. 2013-1; and
consistently comply with the accounting and reporting rules and regulations regarding allocation and utilization of the LDRRMF as stated in the COA Circular No. 2012-002 dated September 12, 2012 and COA Circular No. 2013-1 dated March 25, 2013 to avoid misuse and misappropriation.

We recommended the Management to require the  MPDC and the concerned departments   a) ensure that all the programmed projects for the year included in their Annual Investment Plan be fully implemented during the year by addressing immediately the issues encountered in the execution of the projects and to fortify their response and mitigation projects to address the COVID-19 for the achievement of the desired socio-economic development for the benefit of its constituents; and b) review unimplemented PPAs to ascertain viability of their implementation otherwise reprogram for more viable and significant PPAs in accordance to the socio-economic development and environmental management requirements provided under Section 287 of Republic Act No. 7160 and DILG and DBM  JMC No. 2017-1 and DBM-DOF-DILG JMC No.1.

The Audit Team recommended the Management to a) maximize the utilization of GAD funds through the implementation of GAD-related PPAs addressing the persisting gender issues in the locality; b) periodically update their sex-disaggregated data from their GAD Database to include recent and emerging gender issues to streamline gender analysis, planning and execution; and c) observe strict adherence to the guidelines set forth in the Republic Act No. 9710 and PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC No. 2016-01 amending JMC No. 2013-01 in order to attain the objective for which funds were provided.

We recommended the LSB to:
ensure optimal utilization and full implementation of PPAs under the SEF Budget in compliance with the provisions of Section 272 of RA No. 7160 and DepEd-DBM-DILG Joint Circular No.1 series of 2017 and DepEd-DBM-DILG Joint Circular Nos. 1 and 2 s. 2020; and
include the unexpended balances of prior year’s budget as beginning balance available for appropriation for various programs allowed to be charged to SEF in the current year.

We recommend that the Municipal Accountant record all disbursements to their proper accounts following the illustrative accounting entries provided by the PPSAS to present the account balances fairly.

We recommend that the Municipal Accountant coordinate with the Municipal Treasurer to undertake a thorough review of the discrepancy noted. We further recommend to prepare the following proposed journal entries:
Real Property Tax Receivable 		₱595,792.30
Prior Period Adjustment			₱595,792.30
Special Education Tax Receivable 		₱744,470.37
Prior Period Adjustment			₱744,470.37

We recommend that management submit a valid justification letter for the procurement of materials in excess of needed quantity which is considered unconscionable expenditure as cited under Annex F of COA Circular No. 2012-003 dated October 29, 2012, otherwise may cause the issuance of suspension or disallowance in audit.
We recommend that management ensure that 1% LCPC fund be fully utilized to its programs, projects and activities.

We recommend that management should follow up the production of the SB Resolution requesting for the write-off of these dormant accounts together with the recommendation of the Local Chief Executive. Likewise, the Municipal Accountant have to issue certifications that the municipality has no more records or information, hence the chance to collect is no longer attainable.

We recommend the Municipal Assessor to prioritize the registration and transfer of title of the land to ensure ownership and to fully protect the interest of the Municipality in compliance with Section 39 of PD No.1445. Also, the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Assessor need to reconcile their records of the land owned by the LGU.

We recommend that management should follow up the production of the SB Resolution requesting for the write-off of these dormant accounts together with the recommendation of the Local Chief Executive. Likewise, the Municipal Accountant have to issue certifications that the municipality has no more records or information, hence the chance to collect is no longer attainable.

We recommend that management review the operations of the Waterworks System. Consider increasing the water rates and other applicable fees and charges.
Waterworks Supervisor to check the accuracy and the reliability of the billing system and ensure that the meter matches the electric bill.
Conduct periodic and continuous monitoring and review of the implementation of strategies with the end view that the desired results are achieved.

We recommend management to exert more effort to collect these real property tax delinquencies that have been long overdue in order for the municipality to augment its income that could be used for other projects that would be beneficial to the constituents of the municipality.

We recommend that management require the Municipal Treasurer to prepare a duly certified list showing the name of taxpayers and the amount due and collectible for the year which data should come from the Real Property Tax Account Register (RPTAR) or Taxpayer’s index card maintained at the Municipal Treasurer’s Office and furnish the Municipal Accountant copy of the list to serve as the basis in recording the Real Property Tax Receivable and Special Education Tax Receivable accounts in the books and eventually reported in the Financial Statements.

We have recommended that Municipal Treasurer remit/release the share of the barangays from the basic real property tax and community tax earnings on a quarterly basis within five days after the end of each quarter in consonance with the provisions of Section 271 of RA 7160 of the Local Government Code of 1991 for the barangays to have additional fund to support its Programs, Projects and Activities which are beneficial to its constituents.

We recommended and the Municipal Accountant and the Property Officer agreed to:
Exhaust all efforts to determine the details of the accounts Other Land Improvements and School Buildings so that the Inventory Committee will be able to include the same in the physical count; and
Ensure that PPEs included in the RPCPPE are duly recorded in the Property Cards maintained by the Property Officer and PPE Ledger Cards of the Accountant, and see to it that both records are reconciled.

We recommended and Management agreed to:
Issue demand letters or notices to the AOs with long outstanding unliquidated cash advances, and if warranted, cause the withholding of their salaries pursuant to Section 5.9 of COA Circular 97-002;
Exhaust all efforts to determine the exact address of resigned/retired accountable personnel with unsettled cash advances and send demand letters requiring the immediate liquidation of their cash advances; and
Request for the write-off of unliquidated cash advances of deceased AOs, if warranted, in accordance with COA Circular No. 2016-005.

We recommended and Management agreed to:
Refrain/stop from using fund transfers subsidy from the GF-Proper to fund/pay the PS requirements/expenses of the LEE;
Cause the review and evaluation of the plantilla of personnel of the LEE and its operational expenses, particularly in personnel services, in relation to the revenues earned, to preclude the necessity of funds transfers/subsidy from GF to fund PS expenses; and
Consider and cause the implementation of the appropriate option provided in Section 3.4 of the aforementioned Local Budget Circular 100 when revenues of the LEE cannot fully fund its PS requirements.

We recommended and Management agreed to provide additional personnel to assist in the preparation and submission of financial reports and supporting documents, so that financial reports and its supporting documents are submitted within the required period.

We recommended and Management agreed the following:
Require the Municipal Accountant to:
Take up in the appropriate inventory account the cost of supplies, materials and relief goods procured for distribution, and in the corresponding expense account the subsequent issuance/distribution duly supported by the RIS and RSMI;
Record in the books the inventory of welfare goods for distribution in custody of the Municipality based on the inventory report prepared; and
Prepare and maintain Supplies Ledger Card for each inventory of welfare/ relief goods procured by the Municipality.
Require the Supply/Property Officer to:
Coordinate with the LDRRM Officer to prepare and submit an Inventory List/Report of Welfare Goods for Distribution in custody of the Municipality as of December 31, 2020 in the prescribed format in Annex E; and
Prepare and maintain Stock Cards, Requisition and Issue Slip, and Report of Supplies and Materials Issued for welfare goods.
Cause the submission of the duly validated/approved list of beneficiaries, proof of receipt by and distribution to individual beneficiaries/recipients, and Inventory List/Report of Welfare Goods for Distribution.

We recommended and Management agreed to:
Cause the inclusion of a warranty clause in succeeding Contracts/POs under Emergency Procurement of the Bayanihan Act, to ensure assurance of replacement of/recovery on goods delivered with defects, and/or do not conform to quality standards and technical specifications of the contract; and
Ensure that all Contracts/POs contain complete detailed item specifications pursuant to COA Circular No. 96-010 dated August 15, 1996, to protect the interest of the government.

We recommended and Management agreed to:
The BAC to exert effort to access the GPPB Online Portal and comply with the posting requirements of all projects under the Bayanihan Act undertaken through Negotiated Procurement – Emergency Cases; and
The BAC to coordinate with the GPPB Technical Support Office so that problems in accessing the Online Portal are resolved.

We recommended that Management direct the concerned personnel to:
Prepare the monthly BGCM Utilization Reports on time and post them in three conspicuous places. After which, send a written notice of the posting of reports to the other oversight bodies as provided in Item 4.4 of the DBM LBC No. 125.
Also, furnish the Office of the Auditor copy of the said written notice.

We recommended that Management:
Stop the charging to BGCM Fund the fuel used for purposes that are not in accordance with Item 3.6 of DBM Local Budget Circular No. 125. Instead, spend the amounts to PPAs that can help strengthen the prevention and mitigation measures.
Adhere strictly to the provisions of DBM Local Budget Circular No. 125 in the utilization of the BGCM Fund of the Municipality.

We recommended that Management:
Submit justification/s for the failure of the LGU to comply with the documents required as stipulated in the Memorandum of Agreement signed between the Municipality and DSWD on April 3, 2020.
Moving forward, ensure strict compliance with the roles and responsibilities required for the LGU in accordance with the stipulations set forth in the Memorandum of Agreement signed between the Municipality and other agencies.

We recommended that Management:
Create an Inventory Committee that is composed of two or more employees including the GSO who will conduct physical count of all PPEs which shall be the basis in preparing the RPCPPE.
Direct the Accountant to compute depreciation expense for the Flood Control Systems, Water Supply Systems, and Markets accounts.
Direct the GSO to provide complete information in the RPCPPE, properly classify the PPEs according to their type and assign Property Numbers to all PPEs for easy identification especially during inventory-taking to determine non-existing/missing PPEs.
Refer to COA Circular No. 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020 on the specific guidelines and procedures to assist the LGU in coming up with reliable PPE balances that are verifiable as to existence, condition and accountability.
Direct the Accountant to disclose in the Notes to Financial Statements, the PPEs with their beginning balances found in the accounting system which could not be traced.

We recommended that Management:
Exhaust all possible means to enforce settlement/liquidation for the long overdue receivables; if no chance of being collected, request for authority to write-off from the Commission on Audit.
Direct the Municipal Accountant to conduct review and validation on each of the journal entries made under Advances and Other Receivables accounts to determine the correctness and propriety of the subject entry and to prepare the necessary adjusting entries, when necessary.

We recommended that Management:
Create an Inventory Committee that is composed of two or more employees including the GSO who will conduct physical count of all inventories which shall be the basis in preparing the RPCI.
At the end of each quarter, the Accounting and the GSO should reconcile their records.

We recommended that Management direct the Municipal Accountant to:
Analyze the effects of each transaction and recognize the affected account classification;
Prepare the necessary adjusting journal entries to correct the said errors; and
Ensure the appropriate use of accounts in recording the Municipality’s financial transactions.

We recommended that Management:
Direct the Municipal Treasurer to withhold the salaries of officers and employees with unsettled cash advances, pursuant to Section 37 of Presidential Decree 1445.
For cash advances of an Accountable Officer whose whereabouts could no longer be traced, the agency may file a request for authority to write-off dormant accounts pursuant to COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016.

We recommended that Management:
Comply with the guidelines set in DBM Budget Circulars 2016-4 and 2017-2 in granting personnel benefits such as mid-year bonus, year-end bonus, and cash gift.
Require the concerned separated personnel to refund the amount of ₱33,248.00.

We recommended that Management:
Direct the concerned officials to prepare the quarterly reports on the utilization of the 20% Development Fund as required in the DBM-DOF-DILG Joint Memorandum Circular No. 1.
Also, furnish the Office of the Auditor copy of the said reports.

We recommended that Management:
Submit justification/s regarding the existence of personnel with delinquent loan repayments;
Require the monthly deduction from the monthly compensation of employees the loan amortizations due to GSIS and remit the same in accordance with Sections 14.2 and 14.3 of RA No. 8291 to avoid the occurrence of delinquent loan repayments from members; and
Encourage the employees/personnel listed with delinquent loan repayments to request from the GSIS for loan restructuring to lessen the burden of paying penalties/surcharges, if applicable.

We recommended that Management:
Prioritize the construction of a secured motor pool to shelter all the vehicles and heavy equipment owned by the Municipality to protect them against the elements of nature as well as to safeguard them from possible theft.
Moreover, refer to COA Circular 92-386 on the specific procedures on the disposal of the unserviceable property.

We recommended that Management:
Instruct the Municipal Accountant and Treasurer to withhold/deduct the sum of ₱147,470.19 from the amounts payable to the Contractor.
Moreover, instruct the Municipal Engineer to meticulously prepare and review the ABC of upcoming Infrastructure Projects so that the same technical observation will not occur to those projects.

We recommended that Management:
Require the Municipal Engineer to compel the Contractor to correct the non-functionality of the Icing component, otherwise, Notice of Disallowance shall be issued on the portion paid for the Icing component (Items 313, 314 and 315).
Instruct the Municipal Accountant and Treasurer to withhold the payment of the balance due the contractor.

We recommended that Management require JR3 Construction and Supply to immediately perform rectification work to the affected area to prevent further damage to the completed project and submit Inspection Report with pictures when the noted deficiency is corrected.

We recommended that Management instruct the Accountant to deduct the amount of ₱494,539.94 from the final billing of R. Semilla Construction and Marketing for the construction of Buenavista Community Hospital (Phase III).

We recommended that Management instruct the Accountant to deduct the amount of ₱292,480.43 from the billing of J-R3 Construction and Supply for the Construction of Poblacion 3 Human Resource and Manpower Development Center Phase II.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the:
• Municipal Treasurer to update the entries in her cashbooks on a daily basis to ensure that the daily balances of her accountability are correctly stated; and
• Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Accountant to reconcile their records on cash accounts at least every end of the quarter to ensure the reliability of cashbook balances.
(Reiterated in Audit Observation No. 1, Page No. 34)
observations.managementAction:

observations.reason: Due to multiple adjustments caused by voluminous transactions the Municipal Treasurer was not able to reconcile their balances.

observations.recommendation:

(Reiterated in Audit Observation No. 5, Page No. 39)
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant commented that portion of the amount have been claimed by the suppliers or contractors concerned. observations.reason: Due to the COVID-19 pandemic, the management was not able to fully implement the recommendation.

observations.managementAction: Management was able to submit copies of POs and contracts to the audit team.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Treasurer to transfer the cash of P389,273.73 corresponding to the unutilized balance of the LDRRMF to the GF in compliance with COA Circular No. 2012-002 to avoid dormancy of fund and to ensure that programs, projects and activities under social services are attained.
observations.managementAction: Management already transferred the unutilized balance to the General Fund.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipality, through the Municipal Solid Waste Management Board, secure the approval of its 10-year Solid Waste Management Plan from the National Solid Waste Management Commission and develop and operate a sanitary landfill site as final disposal site pursuant to the provisions of RA No. 9003.

observations.managementAction: Management is already processing the necessary requirements needed, however, the Solid Waste Management plan is not approved yet.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended the Local Chief Executive to:

b)	instruct the Municipal Treasurer to suspend payment of salaries, wages, allowances and processing of monetization of leave credits without complete supporting documents until such time that they have complied with the requirements.

observations.recommendation:

a)	ensure that disbursements were completely supported with documents required under COA Circular No. 2012-001 before payments are made;

b)	submit the corresponding accomplishment report, approved DTR and duly approved authority to render overtime; and

c)	compute the corresponding income/withholding tax on claims for overtime rendered by municipal employees, require them to refund and cause immediate remittance of the same with the Bureau of Internal Revenue.

observations.recommendation:

b)	ensure that all procurements are covered with an approved Annual Procurement Plan and was not meticulously planned; and

c)	install the two units of solar lights to the identified location per plan and require the supplier to refund the cost equivalent of the undelivered one unit solar lights.
observations.managementAction:
No action was taken by the Management.




No action was taken by the Management.
